summary: Exhaustive weakness hunt across Eidetic-Plus: token cost, recall gaps, verified-wrong channels, MCP surface, architecture leverage

result.plan:
# Implementation Plan — connected-brain-loop, single engineer, serial

Ordering rule applied: integrity > accuracy > tokens > product > latency, weighted by value_score and verified risk. 20 confirmed findings compress into 12 commit-sized units; 7 confirmed findings fall below the 12-unit line and are listed as a ranked deferral backlog (nothing dropped). Replay = re-run structured_recall/ask over the existing wave_f sqlite store and diff (answered, verified, answer, supports) per question.

## Units (in commit order)

**1. Mask calendar/measure tokens in `_count_answer`** (finding: count operator returns "2023" as a count; vs=9, integrity-critical)
- Files: `eidetic/smqe/record_ops.py` (2-3 lines inside `_count_answer`, file stays flat).
- Do: mask `_DATE_RE`, `_RACE_TIME_RE`, `_CLOCK_TIME_RE`, `_MONEY_RE`, and standalone `\b(19|20)\d{2}\b` spans before the count-number scan; return `''` if nothing survives, letting `_generic_distinct_count_answer` rescue via existing dispatch.
- Tests: new unit probes (dentist/museum shapes assert no year answer, distinct-count rescue yields "2"); full `tests/test_smqe.py` (156); full suite; wave_f replay — only year-count shapes may change, direction must be correct-answer or abstain, never a new verified string.
- Rollback risk: low; failure mode of the mask is abstain (fail-closed). Rare legit year-sized cardinalities fall to reader.
- Flag: **default-on bug fix** (closes a verified-wrong channel; must not be gateable off).

**2. Subject labels for group superlatives** (finding: "Wednesday" as the friend / label dropped for "who"; vs=8, integrity-high)
- Files: `eidetic/smqe/record_ops.py` (edits inside `_extreme_label_from_atom` and the label-attachment condition at ~2211; offset lines elsewhere to keep the file flat).
- Do: for wh-head who/which-non-time-noun, extract subject preceding the verb; apply weekday/month rejection to subject labels too (sentence-initial "On Wednesday," trap); extend attachment to `which|who|whose`; keep temporal after-text labels only for time-word wh-heads ("which day"); fail closed when no subject label extractable.
- Tests: probes (which-friend -> "Jonas"; who -> "Jonas"; "which day" retains temporal label; "On Wednesday, Jonas ran 9 miles" -> "Jonas"; first-person atoms -> abstain); `tests/test_smqe.py:1930-1976` unchanged; wave_f replay diff.
- Rollback risk: low-medium; regex subject capture is the risk surface — the weekday/month rejection on subjects is mandatory, and first-person who-superlatives convert verified-bare-measurement to abstention (correct direction, small recall delta).
- Flag: **default-on bug fix** (verified-wrong channel).

**3. Scope `recall_trace`** (finding: cross-namespace trace leak + pooled-thread stale reads; vs=7, integrity-high)
- Files: `eidetic/engine.py` (per-scope bounded snapshot dict, last-1-per-scope, same lock), `eidetic/mcp_server.py`, `eidetic/api.py` (namespace/agent/project params, resolve via `_scope()`), skip thread-local fallback on the cross-call surface.
- Tests: update `tests/test_brain_spine.py:227` and `tests/test_mcp_server.py:210`; new tests: interleaved asks in two scopes return only the matching scope's trace; thread-pool simulation proves no stale foreign trace; bound check on the snapshot dict.
- Rollback risk: low; behavior change confined to introspection surface (no-arg call after non-default-namespace ask now returns `{}` — documented contract). No answer-path involvement.
- Flag: **default-on bug fix** (restores the documented "reads never cross namespaces" guarantee).

**4. Plural-enumeration operator** (finding: 1-of-3 single-support answer ships verified for "which countries have I visited?"; accuracy-high)
- Files: `eidetic/smqe/qa_ops.py` (new operator — `record_ops.py` untouched), `eidetic/config.py` (flag).
- Do: when the wh-head is plural/enumerative and multiple distinct slot values from distinct records score within a tie band, enumerate distinct values each with its own support; each element must pass verify (multi-record supports satisfy the existing witness rule); if elements cannot each be verified, fall through to today's path.
- Tests: flag-off byte-identical (suite green); flag-on probes (countries shape -> enumerated multi-support answer, never single-support); wave_f replay flag-on vs flag-off diff for candidate-lift measurement.
- Rollback risk: contained by flag; the risk is over-enumeration (bundling non-answers), bounded by per-element verification.
- Flag: **flag-gated default-off** (new answer channel; promote only after replay + eventual live A/B).

**5. Gate `extract_claims` on `CLAIM_EXTRACTION`** (finding: claim LLM calls fire and are provably discarded when flag off; vs=7)
- Files: `eidetic/engine.py` (`_extract`, one-line condition using the exact same flag as engine.py:1638; gate claim calls only, never the edges calls sharing the try block).
- Tests: counting-mock test: flag off -> 0 `extract_claims` calls, edges calls unchanged; flag on -> byte-identical claims; `tests/test_consolidation_claim_flag.py` green.
- Rollback risk: very low; flag-on path byte-identical; flag-off historical write-cost baselines become non-comparable (intended correction).
- Flag: **default-on bug fix** (dead-call elimination, ~50% extraction spend in every CLAIM_EXTRACTION=0 config; makes ablation cost accounting honest).

**6. Sub-claim grounding early-stop + advice-rescue dedup** (findings 1 and 3 merged: one concern — sub-claim verification must never pay for verdicts that cannot change; vs=7+5)
- Files: `eidetic/retrieval.py` (new `_claim_grounded` helper used by span-NLI at ~4748 and CoVe at ~4731; same-`_support_norm`-as-whole-answer skip in the advice rescue at ~4706, reusing the line-4688 citations' contradiction labels).
- Do: free local checks first (`_aggregation_proof_support`, `_extractive_entailment`), then model NLI in whole-answer-entailed-first fused order stopping at first entailment; demotion still requires zero entailment over the full set. Hard carve-outs per verified risk: the advice-anchor rescue path (4706-4721) must NOT use the early-stop helper (it consumes CONTRADICTION over the full set); under BATCH_NLI keep one batch per claim (assert round-trip count does not increase).
- Tests: counting-mock NLI client — entailing candidate at position k costs exactly k pairs; zero-entailment claim exhausts the full set then demotes; single-sentence advice rescue issues 0 duplicate pairs; BATCH_NLI round-trip count unchanged; full suite; wave_f replay must show zero answered/verified diffs (semantics-preserving by construction).
- Rollback risk: low-medium; a buggy early exit causes false demotion (fail-closed, never verified-wrong); the flake-reroll loss on single-sentence advice is integrity-neutral-to-positive.
- Flag: **default-on** (order-independent existence check, output-identical); include a kill-switch env for one release.

**7. Honor FAST_VERIFY under BATCH_NLI (wave batching)** (finding: batch branch has no entailment cap and the shipping profile sets both flags; vs=7)
- Files: `eidetic/retrieval.py` (`_verify_candidates` batch branch, ~4521-4543).
- Do: wave 1 = extractive pass + one batch over top-N non-extractive by fused score (N = `verify_citation_cap`); any entailment -> rest NEUTRAL (exact shipped FAST_VERIFY semantics); zero entailments -> batch remainder before deciding (abstain decision computed over full set, never weakened); guard: escalate to wave 2 whenever wave 1 contains any contradiction (protects the advice-rescue kill), and keep full width for sub-claim invocations (mostly mooted by unit 6 landing first).
- Tests: counting-mock — wave counts per scenario (entailed / zero-entailed / contradiction-escalation); abstention-equality test vs full-width on synthetic stores; flags-off paths byte-identical; full suite.
- Rollback risk: medium; adds one serial NLI round-trip on abstain-bound questions (measure in replay timing); marginally fewer entailed citations feeds abstention_v2 and FSRS reinforcement (fail-closed direction).
- Flag: **no new flag** — gated by the already-shipped FAST_VERIFY+BATCH_NLI combination; change only activates where the profile explicitly opted in.

**8. Combined extraction call + honest call budget** (finding: every window paid twice for edges+claims, budget counts windows not calls; severity critical, vs=7)
- Files: `eidetic/dashscope_client.py` (combined window method, max_tokens 4096), `eidetic/engine.py` (`_extract`, `_planned_extract_windows`), `eidetic/config.py` (`EXTRACT_COMBINED=0`).
- Do: one prompt per window returning `{"triples":[...],"claims":[...]}`, parsed by existing `_parse_triples`/`_parse_claims`/`_salvage_json_objects`, feeding both downstream pipelines unchanged. Budget accounting counts effective calls (windows x active extractors) **coupled to the flag** — flag-off accounting stays byte-identical (the live 90 was de facto tuned against 2x spend).
- Tests: counting mock asserts combined mode issues exactly half the calls and byte-identical records/claims/events/edges given equivalent model output; salvage unit tests for mixed/truncated combined objects; budget test at 90; getattr fallback preserved for non-dashscope clients; flag-off suite byte-identical.
- Rollback risk: contained by flag; residual risks are combined-prompt quality (needs live A/B, quota-blocked) and coupled failure of one window losing both channels (salvage-mitigated, unit-tested).
- Flag: **flag-gated default-off**.

**9. Persistent extraction-result cache** (finding: temp-0 extraction re-pays ~120k tokens/row on re-ingestion while embeds are cached; vs=7)
- Files: new `eidetic/extract_cache.py` mirroring `embed_cache.py` (per-thread-connection WAL), `eidetic/dashscope_client.py` (wrap `_extract_edges_window`/`_extract_claims_window`/combined), `eidetic/config.py` (`EXTRACT_RESULT_CACHE=0`), `bench/run.py` (`_MANIFEST_ENV` registration).
- Do: key = sha256(model_id + full system+user prompt text + window text) — prompt-hash, not a manual revision tag, per verified risk; cache raw model JSON, parse on read; never cache errors or moderation skips.
- Tests: counting mock — second consolidation of identical content issues 0 extraction calls, byte-identical triples/claims; prompt-text change -> miss; thread-pool concurrency smoke; manifest registration test.
- Rollback risk: low with prompt-hash keying; model-alias drift is the same accepted risk as the shipped embed cache. Impact is real but confined to reused DATA_DIRs.
- Flag: **flag-gated default-off**.

**10. `verify_citation` LRU memo** (finding: SMQE claim backend executes and verify-fails twice per ask, re-paying identical NLI; vs=7; also backstops any residual duplicate pairs from units 6/7)
- Files: `eidetic/retrieval.py` or shared helper consumed by `eidetic/smqe/verify.py`, `eidetic/config.py` (flag).
- Do: bounded LRU keyed (sha256(actual premise string), normalized-hypothesis hash, verify model) — premise hash, not content_hash alone, because `_ground_truth` can fall back to mutable `rec.text`; memoize successful verdicts only. Do NOT take the alternative "skip claim backend on run 2" — verified as a real behavior delta, not a pure dedup.
- Tests: counting mock — repeat identical triple costs 1 client call; only successes memoized; bound respected; synthetic-store SMQE-fail-then-fallback ask pays 0 duplicate NLI; flag-off byte-identical; audit tests that count `client.nli` calls.
- Rollback risk: contained by flag; sticky temp-0 flake within LRU lifetime is the known trade (symmetric, bounded).
- Flag: **flag-gated default-off** (changes retry semantics = new behavior).

**11. Rerank on query-centered spans** (finding: 50 x 4000-char full texts per uncached ask, the largest read-path stream; vs=7)
- Files: `eidetic/retrieval.py` (`_finalize` ~4029-4031), `eidetic/config.py` (`RERANK_SPAN_INPUT=0`).
- Do: feed `_raw_query_centered_span(text, query, max_chars~1200-1600)` per shortlist doc, **setting long_threshold_chars ~ max_chars** (verified implementation nit — otherwise the cut does not materialize). Ranking-only; verification untouched.
- Tests: flag-off byte-identical; flag-on unit test of span-builder invocation params; offline replay harness over wave_f store comparing final_topk overlap + answered/abstain deltas flag-on vs off. Promotion to profile requires the dev-split recall gate (live, quota-blocked — no default flip in this plan).
- Rollback risk: contained by flag; known tail risk is lexically-disjoint long docs falling out of top-k (abstain direction, never verified-wrong).
- Flag: **flag-gated default-off** + dev recall gate before promotion.

**12. Importance/affect scoring dedup + `SLEEP_SCORE_IMPORTANCE`** (finding: score_importance fully overwritten by score_affect; product sleep pays N useless flash calls; vs=6)
- Files: `eidetic/engine.py` (consolidate_pending), `eidetic/lifecycle.py` (sleep), `eidetic/config.py`.
- Do: skip score_importance when the affect branch will score the record; except-handler falls back to score_importance + the 0.45/0.55 salience recompute; clients whose score_affect omits 'importance' still get score_importance (verified three-path equivalence requirement). Add `SLEEP_SCORE_IMPORTANCE` default `'1'` (today's behavior). Profile flip to `'0'` is explicitly NOT in this commit — it changes forgetting-side inputs (FSRS initial stability, salience pruning) and must be a separate A/B-able change.
- Tests: mock-client equivalence across all three paths (affect success / affect raises / affect-without-importance-key), asserting equal final importance+salience with one fewer call; lifecycle.sleep byte-identical at default; flag-'0' path test.
- Rollback risk: low given the three-path tests; the risky part (profile flip) is deliberately excluded.
- Flag: dedup **default-on** (behavior-identical, test-proven); new sleep flag **default preserves today**.

## Deferred confirmed findings (next wave, ranked — not lost)

13. Self-repair dead-end (repair MCP tool + POST /api/repair, dry_run default) — first product item of the next wave; needs a flag/rate-limit on apply=true (remote re-embed trigger) and awareness of the pre-existing rebuild/ingest race.
14. /api/truth_ledger drops as_of — one-line route parity fix; can ride any api.py commit; document is_current stays present-time-relative.
15. recall(prove=True) triple-ships citations and ignores limit — citation-refs variant; breaks `tests/test_flagships.py:26` shape + proofs docstring promise, so it needs its own commit.
16. GET /api/memories unpaginated — mirror MCP paging; must land with web UI paging (`eidetic/web/index.html:549`) and `tests/test_api_scope.py:88-91` updates in the same change.
17. ingest_many unreachable from transports — valuable but requires aligning ingest_many dedup (per-valid_at + intra-batch seen-set) and fixing the orphan-blob put-before-embed order first; not a thin exposure commit.
18. assemble_context five redundant corpus scans — trivially safe in-method hoist (~85-110ms/ask at 5k records); do the hoist only, not the retrieve()-threading half (salience-prune and precomputed-candidates traps verified).
19. Line-aligned extraction chunker — sequenced after unit 8 because `_planned_extract_windows` and `bench/run.py:286` must mirror the chunker behind the same flag; carry-over must cap at the overlap budget or the token win inverts.

## Rejected findings (with refutation, one line each)

- **Procedural recall tier (verified-helpful/FSRS write-only on answer path)** — core observation survives but the cost claim is wrong: exact-hash semantic cache already serves verbatim repeats free and verbatim-anchor verify is zero-model-call; the real opportunity (paraphrases, post-write repeats) needs its own design, not a fix-sized change.
- **Namespace-global cache version bump collapses hit rate** — real cost problem, unsound remedy: deferred consolidation means a contradicting write leaves no claim/hash trace, so the proposed revalidation serves stale verified=True answers — a verified-wrong channel, disqualifying under the hard rules.
- **SMQE answers never vector-cached** — proposed plan-derived key under-determines answers (before/after filter variants collapse to identical keys; record_ops reads the raw query 376 times), so the cache would serve verified-wrong cross-answers; hit-rate and cost savings both overstated.

Key file paths: `/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/eidetic/smqe/record_ops.py`, `/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/eidetic/smqe/qa_ops.py`, `/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/eidetic/retrieval.py`, `/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/eidetic/engine.py`, `/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/eidetic/dashscope_client.py`, `/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/eidetic/mcp_server.py`, `/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/eidetic/api.py`, `/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/eidetic/config.py`, `/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/eidetic/lifecycle.py`.

confirmed.title: Span-NLI and CoVe claim checks re-verify the entire candidate set per claim, but only the boolean 'any entailment' is consumed
confirmed.evidence: retrieval.py:4748-4756 (span NLI) and 4731-4740 (CoVe) call `_c, claim_entailed = self._verify_candidates(candidates, claim, True, ...)` once per sentence claim; the returned citations `_c` are discarded and only `claim_entailed == 0` is read. `_verify_candidates` (4513-4582) verifies ALL candidates (final_topk=10): in default config that is up to 10 serial `client.nli` chat calls per claim (premise[:6000]+hyp[:2000], dashscope_client.py:755); in BATCH_NLI mode it is one request carrying up to 10 pairs at premise[:3000] each (dashscope_client.py:778). It also rebuilds `_citation_snippet` for every candidate and re-runs `_aggregation_proof_support` (re-parsing the query) per claim, all discarded. A 4-claim answer over 10 candidates pays up to 40 NLI pairs where the demote/keep decision needs at most 'first entailing candidate' per claim.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add a dedicated `_claim_grounded(candidates, claim, query, at)` used by span-NLI and CoVe: (1) run the free local checks first (`_aggregation_proof_support`, `_extractive_entailment` across all candidates); (2) issue model NLI in whole-answer-entailed-first, fused-score order and stop at the FIRST entailment; (3) only if none entails after exhausting all candidates, return ungrounded (demote). Semantics are exactly preserved: 'exists an entailing candidate' is order-independent, and demotion still requires zero entailment over the full set, so no verified-wrong channel can open — the change only skips NLI pairs that can no longer change the verdict. Skip building citations/snippets entirely on this path.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens: with SPAN_NLI/CoVe on (the all-on dominance profile), typical grounded multi-claim answers drop from ~30-40 NLI pairs to ~4-8 (~10-30k chars of premise text saved per question); latency: in default serial mode this removes dozens of sequential governed model round-trips per multi-sentence answer.

verdict.risk: Three surfaces: (1) the shared helper must not be reused for the advice-anchor rescue path (retrieval.py:4706-4721), which consumes CONTRADICTION labels over the full citation set — early-stopping there would let a contradicted rescue land; (2) a buggy early exit on the no-entailment path would cause false demotions/abstentions (fail-closed accuracy loss, not verified-wrong); (3) in BATCH_NLI mode the current cost is already one round-trip per claim, so naive serial first-entailment probing could increase round-trips/latency even while cutting tokens — batching must be preserved when that flag is on. No output-visible behavior change otherwise: citations come from the whole-answer pass and the consumed boolean is identical.

confirmed.title: FAST_VERIFY is silently dead under BATCH_NLI: the batch branch has no entailment cap, and the shipping metabolism profile enables both
confirmed.evidence: `_verify_candidates` branch order at retrieval.py:4521-4543 is `if s.batch_nli_enabled: ... elif s.fast_verify_enabled: ...` — the batch path sends EVERY non-extractively-proven candidate (up to final_topk=10 pairs, premise[:3000]+hyp[:1200] each ≈ 10k tokens/pass) with no short-circuit, while the fast_verify cap (verify_citation_cap=3) sits unreachable in the elif. METABOLISM_PROFILE sets both "BATCH_NLI": "1" and "FAST_VERIFY": "1" (config.py:82-83, comment: 'stop verifying once enough entailments are found'), so the product profile pays full-width verification on every answer despite explicitly opting into capped verification.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Make the batch path honor the already-declared cap semantics via wave batching: wave 1 = extractive pass over all candidates (free) + one nli_batch over the top-N non-extractive candidates by fused score (N = verify_citation_cap or slightly above); if wave 1 yields at least one entailment, remaining candidates stay NEUTRAL exactly as FAST_VERIFY already defines; if wave 1 yields zero entailments, batch the remainder before deciding, so the verified/abstain decision is computed over the full set and never weakens. This is the same integrity trade the shipped, reviewed FAST_VERIFY flag already makes — it just stops being ignored when batching is on.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens: ~60-70% cut of whole-answer verification input on the profile path (10 pairs -> ~3 typical, saving ~6-7k premise chars per answered question, and multiplied again by every cascade escalation and advice-rescue pass that reuses _verify_candidates).

verdict.risk: Three regression surfaces. (1) Latency: batch_nli's purpose is one round trip; the two-wave design adds a second serial NLI request precisely on zero-entailment (abstain-bound) questions, and on every span-NLI/CoVe sub-claim that fails to ground. (2) Contradiction visibility: today's profile verifies full-width, so a late CONTRADICTION kills the advice rescue (retrieval.py:4714); a wave-1 cap that stops at first entailment can miss it and let a rescue survive that today dies — same trade shipped FAST_VERIFY already makes, but it IS a relaxation vs current profile behavior; cheap guard: escalate to wave 2 whenever wave 1 contains any contradiction, or keep full width for sub-claim invocations. (3) Fewer entailed citations lowers abstention_v2's entail signal (max nli_score over entailed, line 4591) and shrinks the set engine reconsolidation reinforces — marginally more borderline abstentions (fail-closed) and less FSRS reinforcement. Also note impact sizing: SMQE-answered questions bypass this path entirely (smqe/verify.py is separate), so the fleet-wide token cut is confined to retrieval-fallback answers plus their cascade/advice/CoVe/span re-invocations, smaller than the finding's 'every answered question' framing.

confirmed.title: Advice rescue re-issues the byte-identical whole-answer NLI batch when the advice answer is a single sentence
confirmed.evidence: retrieval.py:4706-4713: on `entailed == 0` for an advice query, `sentence_claims = [cl for cl in _sentences(text) ...][:5]` and each claim goes through `_verify_candidates(candidates, cl, True, ...)`. For a one-sentence answer `_sentences(text) == [text]` (verified: `_sentences('You could try ...') == [text]`), so the rescue immediately re-verifies the exact (premise, hypothesis) pairs that the whole-answer pass at line 4688 just judged as entailed==0 — up to 10 duplicate NLI calls (default serial config) or one duplicate 10-pair batch, with temperature=0 guaranteeing the same labels. Unlike span-NLI (guarded by `len(claims) > 1` at 4750), the advice rescue has no same-as-whole-answer guard.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Skip any rescue claim whose `_support_norm(cl)` equals `_support_norm(text)`: its verdict is already known (entailed==0) and its contradiction labels are already present in the `citations` computed at line 4688, so reuse those for the rescue-killing contradiction check. Outcome is byte-identical (same deterministic pairs), only the duplicate model pass is removed. General grounds: pure dedup of an identical verification, no question/dataset matching.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens+latency: eliminates one full verification pass (~10 NLI pairs, ~40k chars serial / ~10k tokens) on every single-sentence advice answer that failed whole-answer NLI — exactly the abstain-bound answers where the current path pays double for zero information.

verdict.risk: Under API nondeterminism (temp-0 is near- but not strictly deterministic), the duplicate pass sometimes acted as a re-roll that rescued a single-sentence advice answer the first pass judged neutral; removing it flips those flake-rescues to unverified/abstain — integrity-neutral-to-positive but can marginally lower measured advice accuracy on live runs. Implementation risk: the guard must be a claim-level skip only; wiring the cached whole-answer citations in as a rescue verdict (misreading the sketch) would be a bug. Regression surface is confined to the advice-rescue branch of retrieval.answer(); multi-sentence advice answers and span-NLI are untouched.

confirmed.title: SMQE claim-backend executes and verify-fails twice per ask: engine-level run and answer()'s candidate-level run share identical inputs, re-paying the same verify_citation NLI calls
confirmed.evidence: engine.py:965 runs full-store SMQE; when it returns None the retrieval fallback calls `retriever.answer`, which unconditionally re-runs SMQE via `_structured_answer_from_candidates` (retrieval.py:4668). The claim backend's inputs are identical in both runs: `plan_query(query, at)` is deterministic and `claims = store.active_claims_at(at, scope)` comes from the FULL store regardless of the records argument (smqe/engine.py:82-86), and `execute_plan` tries claims first (executor.py:18-20). So whenever the engine-level run produced a claim-backend result that failed verification (verify.py:139-140 returns None AFTER paying 1-2 `verify_citation` model NLI calls per support, verify.py:115-120), the candidate-level run reproduces the byte-identical result and re-pays the byte-identical NLI calls before failing identically and falling through to the reader.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add a small bounded LRU memo on the Retriever for `verify_citation` keyed by (rec.content_hash, normalized-hypothesis hash, verify model): temperature-0 NLI is deterministic and content_hash is immutable WORM content, so a memo hit returns the same verdict the model would — verification strength is unchanged by construction, and no invalidation is needed. This also collapses the advice-rescue duplicate (finding 3) and repeated strict-hypothesis checks across SMQE runs. Alternative narrower fix: have engine.ask pass a 'claim backend already executed for this (query, at, scope)' marker so `_structured_answer_from_candidates` skips `execute_claim_op` and only re-tries the record backend (the only part whose inputs actually differ).
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens+latency: removes 1-2 duplicated NLI calls per support on every ask where the claim backend fires but fails verification (the common path into the reader fallback), plus a full duplicated execute_plan pass over all store claims (CPU) on every fallback ask.

verdict.risk: Memo fix: pins the first NLI verdict for identical (record, hypothesis, model) triples for the LRU lifetime — a transiently flaky temperature-0 verdict (wrong 'neutral' or wrong 'entailment') becomes sticky within the process instead of being re-rolled; symmetric in both directions and bounded, but it changes retry semantics. Key on a hash of the actual premise string, not content_hash alone, because _ground_truth (retrieval.py:3295) falls back to mutable rec.text when the substrate lookup fails; memoize only successful verdicts. Tests that mock/count client.nli calls may break on call counts. The alternative marker fix is riskier than the finder states: today a verify-failed claim result also shadows the record backend in the candidate-level run (executor.py:18-21), so skipping execute_claim_op in run 2 newly exposes the record backend over candidates — verify-gated (no integrity hole) but a real behavior/accuracy delta that needs measurement, not a pure dedup.

confirmed.title: Cross-encoder rerank ships 50 full record texts (up to 4000 chars each) on every uncached ask — the largest single token stream on the read path, unbounded by the span discipline used everywhere else
confirmed.evidence: `_finalize` (retrieval.py:4029-4031) builds `shortlist = ranked[:max(s.rerank_depth, s.final_topk)]` (defaults: 50) and sends `docs = [c.record.text or c.record.summary or '' ...]` — full record texts — to `client.rerank`, which truncates each doc only at 4000 chars (dashscope_client.py:813). RERANK_ENABLED defaults to 1 (config.py:323-325), so every non-reflex, non-cache, non-SMQE ask pays up to ~200k chars (~50k tokens) of qwen3-rerank input. The repo already established the bounded-span discipline for model inputs elsewhere ('do not send a 500k-character transcript to NLI' — `_bounded_proof_premise`, retrieval.py:4417-4433; reader raw blocks capped via `_raw_query_centered_span` at 4323-4329); rerank is the one remaining consumer of unbounded full-text.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Behind a new flag, feed the reranker `_raw_query_centered_span(text, query, max_chars≈1200-1600)` per shortlist doc instead of the raw 4000-char prefix — the cross-encoder scores query-passage relevance, and the query-centered span is precisely the passage evidence; a 4000-char PREFIX can even miss the relevant region of a long record that a centered span captures. Promote only via the repo's existing dev-split recall gate (config.py:259 convention), keeping the default byte-identical until measured. Verification is untouched (rerank is ranking-only), so verify-or-abstain integrity is unaffected.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens: ~60-70% cut of the biggest read-path token stream (~200k -> ~60-80k chars per ask); latency: proportionally smaller rerank request on every ask; accuracy risk bounded by the dev recall gate before promotion.

verdict.risk: Ranking regressions on long records where the lexical query-centered span picker misses the semantically relevant region: the no-match fallback returns text[:max_chars] (retrieval.py:2328/2350), a SHORTER prefix than today's 4000-char client truncation, so lexically-disjoint but relevant docs could drop out of the top-k; this changes which candidates flow to MMR/adaptive-k/hopfield ordering and to verify-or-abstain (more abstentions possible, never verified-wrong). Implementation nit: _raw_query_centered_span defaults long_threshold_chars=12000 and passes texts below that through untouched, so the caller must also set long_threshold_chars≈max_chars or the cut mostly does not materialize. Both risks are contained by the flag-off byte-identical default and dev recall gate; the residual risk is promoting on a dev split too small to expose the long-doc lexical-miss tail. Token severity is slightly overstated: the ~50k tokens are cheap qwen3-rerank input, not reader-LLM tokens — latency and stream-size wins are real, dollar impact is more modest and workload-dependent (short-claim-heavy stores see far less than 60-70%).

confirmed.title: assemble_context performs five separate full-corpus active_records_at scans (plus a duplicate events_in_scope) on every reader-path ask
confirmed.evidence: Within one `assemble_context` call, `self.store.active_records_at(at..., scope)` is recomputed independently for scratchpad (retrieval.py:4134), user evidence (4184), assistant evidence (4197), temporal audit (4212), and list audit (4238) — all five audit channels are ON in the metabolism/dominance profile (config.py:42-48). Each call is an O(corpus) store scan with per-record visibility/validity checks. `retrieve()` already scanned the same corpus at 3338, and `events_in_scope` is fetched both at 3350 (retrieve) and 4112 (assemble_context) for the same (namespace, scope, at).
confirmed.fix_sketch: Hoist one `active = self.store.active_records_at(at if at is not None else now(), scope)` to the top of assemble_context and pass it to all five audit builders (they already accept the record list); optionally thread the corpus/events computed in retrieve() through to answer()/assemble_context since (at, scope) are identical within one ask. Pure refactor — the same inputs reach every matcher, outputs byte-identical, no model-call or verification change; record_ops untouched.
confirmed.expected_impact: latency: removes four redundant O(corpus) scans plus one duplicate event fetch per answered question; on multi-thousand-record namespaces this is the dominant non-model CPU cost of context assembly and directly reduces time-to-first-reader-token.

verdict.risk: In-method hoist is near-zero risk: no store mutation occurs between the five call sites (verified — _record_context_region_hints writes only threadlocal telemetry), so a single snapshot is byte-identical; when at=None the hoist collapses several microseconds-apart now() snapshots into one, which is semantically equivalent (answer() always passes a resolved at). The optional 'thread corpus from retrieve()' half has two regression traps: (1) retrieve() salience-prunes the corpus at retrieval.py:3343-3344 before building its records dict — threading the post-prune list would silently shrink audit-channel visibility, so the pre-prune list must be threaded; (2) answer() supports precomputed candidates (skips retrieve) and the bench adapter calls assemble_context directly, so the parameter must default to None with a fallback self-fetch or those paths break. A latent risk: any FUTURE audit builder that mutates the store mid-assembly would see a stale snapshot, but none do today.
verdict.refutation: Finding survives with one correction: it claims all five audit channels are ON in the metabolism/dominance profile (config.py:42-48), but SCRATCHPAD is not in METABOLISM_PROFILE and defaults to '0' (config.py:567), so only four of the five scans execute in-profile (USER_EVIDENCE_CONTEXT, ASSISTANT_EVIDENCE_CONTEXT, TEMPORAL_EVIDENCE_AUDIT, LIST_AUDIT — config.py:42-47). Also the cost mechanism is slightly misdescribed: visibility/validity filtering happens in SQL (store.py:246-251), and the O(corpus) cost is dominated by per-row pydantic model_validate_json deserialization — the redundancy claim holds regardless. Measured offline: ~21-28ms per call at 5k small records, so ~85-110ms redundant per ask (scales with corpus and record size), which is a real but modest latency win relative to multi-second reader LLM calls. All six call sites confirmed (retrieval.py:3338, 4134, 4184, 4197, 4212, 4238) with identical (t, scope) per ask; duplicate events_in_scope confirmed (3350 gated on EVENT_RANKING=1-in-profile, 4112 unconditional).

confirmed.title: Every extraction window is transmitted twice: edges and claims are separate LLM calls over identical window text (and the call budget counts windows, not calls)
confirmed.evidence: eidetic/engine.py:1475-1482: for each pending record, `_extract` calls `bounded(rec.text, max_windows=allow)` AND `claim_bounded(rec.text, max_windows=allow)` (or `extract_edges` + `extract_claims`). Both walk the SAME windows: eidetic/dashscope_client.py:624-632 and 676-684 each call `chunk_text(text, s.extract_chunk_chars, s.extract_chunk_overlap)` with identical params, then send each ~4000-char window (~1,000+ input tokens) with its own system prompt and its own `max_tokens=2048` completion (dashscope_client.py:643-649 and 689-698). So the dominant term of the ~120k write tokens/row — the haystack text itself — is paid twice. Additionally, the CONSOLIDATION_EXTRACT_CALL_BUDGET plan counts windows once (`_planned_extract_windows`, engine.py:1394-1408) while each allowed window issues up to 2 LLM calls, so a budget of 90 actually admits up to 180 calls — the budget's own deadline/cost rationale (config.py:63 'cap huge sleep batches before timeout collapse') is off by 2x.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add a flag-gated combined extraction call (e.g. EXTRACT_COMBINED=0 default off): one prompt per window returning {"triples":[...],"claims":[...]}, parsed by the existing `_parse_triples`/`_parse_claims`/`_salvage_json_objects` machinery and fed into the two existing downstream pipelines unchanged (dedupe_triples, dedupe_claim_dicts, claims_for_record). Raise the combined call's max_tokens to 4096 so dense windows don't truncate. Independently, make `window_plan` count effective calls (windows x active extractors) so the budget semantics match spend. Offline equivalence tests: a counting mock client asserts combined mode issues exactly half the calls and, given equivalent model output, produces byte-identical records/claims/events/graph edges; the salvage path is unit-tested for both keys. General grounds only — no dataset knowledge; verify-or-abstain untouched (extraction feeds enrichment, never the answer gate); record_ops.py untouched.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens: ~40-45% reduction in per-row write tokens on full-consolidation runs (window text + prompt sent once instead of twice per window); also halves extraction wall-clock pressure against CONSOLIDATION_EXTRACT_DEADLINE_SEC (latency), and makes the 90-call budget mean what it says.

verdict.risk: 1) Combined single-prompt extraction may degrade per-task quality vs two specialized prompts (fewer canonical triples, non-verbatim proof_atoms get dropped by the containment check at claim_extraction.py:140) — an accuracy-recall regression in graph/event/claims channels that only a live A/B can rule out, and live runs are quota-blocked; flag-gated default-off contains this. 2) Failure coupling: one truncated or moderation-rejected combined completion now loses BOTH triples and claims for that window, where today the two calls fail independently; salvage mitigates truncation but must classify mixed objects (unit tests needed, though required-key filters already disambiguate). 3) The budget re-semantics piece (count windows x active extractors) halves effective window coverage under the live profile's CONSOLIDATION_EXTRACT_CALL_BUDGET=90 if landed while combined mode is off — the 90 was de facto tuned against 2x spend — so it must land coupled with combined mode or with the constant retuned, else long-haystack enrichment coverage regresses. 4) Non-dashscope/mock clients need the combined method or the existing getattr fallback path preserved.

confirmed.title: extract_claims LLM calls fire even when CLAIM_EXTRACTION=0 — output is provably discarded (reproduced offline)
confirmed.evidence: engine.py:1477-1482 calls `claim_bounded`/`claim_extract` unconditionally (only `callable()` checks), but `extracted_claims` is consumed at exactly one site, engine.py:1638-1639, which is guarded by `if self.settings.claim_extraction_enabled:`. Reproduced offline with a counting fake client (.venv/bin/python, no API): `claim_extraction_enabled=False` -> consolidate_pending fires 1 edges call AND 1 claims call, report shows claims_extracted=0. The existing test tests/test_consolidation_claim_flag.py:56-63 only asserts no claims are WRITTEN, so it cannot catch the wasted call. This also inflates the metabolism-off ablation's write-cost row (config.py:337-340 says CLAIM_EXTRACTION=0 exists precisely to 'measure what consolidation-written structure actually earns') — the ablation currently pays ~full claim-extraction cost while getting none of the structure, corrupting the cost side of the delta.
confirmed.fix_sketch: In `_extract`, gate the claim calls on `self.settings.claim_extraction_enabled` (one-line condition alongside the callable checks). This is a pure dead-call elimination: outputs are byte-identical because the discarded list was already empty-equivalent downstream. Add a counting-mock test asserting zero extract_claims calls when the flag is off and unchanged claims when on. Shrinks engine.py by nothing but adds no growth to record_ops.py; no integrity surface touched.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens: ~50% of extraction spend eliminated in every CLAIM_EXTRACTION=0 configuration (ablations, cost-lean deployments), and the metabolism ablation's token accounting becomes honest.

verdict.risk: Very low. Flag-on path is byte-identical. Flag-off path changes only in side effects of the removed calls: fewer _degraded('extract-edges') events (claim-call exceptions can no longer occur), shorter consolidation wall-time under consolidation_extract_deadline_sec (marginally more records may now finish before the deadline in flag-off runs, changing timeout/defer counts), and historical flag-off write-cost baselines become non-comparable — which is the intended correction. Gate must use the exact same flag as engine.py:1638 to avoid drift. One nuance the fix must preserve: gate only the claim calls, not the edges calls sharing the same try block.

confirmed.title: No persistent extraction-result cache: identical window text is re-extracted at temperature 0.0 on every re-run, while embeddings already have exactly this cache
confirmed.evidence: Embeddings are served from PersistentEmbedCache keyed (model, dim, sha256(text)) (dashscope_client.py:504-528, embed_cache.py:38-48, EMBED_CACHE default '1' at config.py:209), so repeat ingestion of the same text embeds for free. The extraction calls that dominate write cost have no equivalent: `_extract_edges_window` (dashscope_client.py:634-668) and `_extract_claims_window` (686-703) are deterministic requests (temperature=0.0, fixed prompts) yet always hit the network. Dev iteration loops re-ingest the same LongMemEval/LoCoMo haystack text across runs (the whole run history in artifacts/ is exactly this loop), and `clear_namespace` (engine.py:392-428) resets scoped state without touching client-level caches — so the raw material for a hit is already recurring; only extraction re-pays ~120k tokens/row every time.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add a flag-gated (EXTRACT_RESULT_CACHE=0 default off) persistent cache mirroring PersistentEmbedCache: key = (model_id, prompt_revision_tag, sha256(window_text)), value = the raw model JSON, parsed through the existing parser on read so parser fixes still apply. Store only successful responses; content-moderation skips and errors are never cached (no fail-open channel). Offline equivalence test: counting mock client — second consolidation of identical content issues 0 extraction calls and yields byte-identical triples/claims. General grounds: content-hash keyed, dataset-agnostic, same discipline as the shipped embed cache; cached output is a prior REAL model response for byte-identical input, so no verified-wrong channel can be created.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens: near-100% write-token elimination on repeat ingestion of unchanged content — the dominant spend pattern of the quota-blocked dev loop — plus intra-run savings wherever identical session text recurs across namespaces; also large latency win on re-runs.

verdict.risk: Stale-cache footgun: the sketched manual prompt_revision_tag silently serves old-prompt extractions if not bumped, which could contaminate prompt A/B attribution — key should be sha256 of the actual system+user prompt text instead. Model-alias pinning: qwen-plus upgrades upstream while cached responses reflect the old snapshot (same accepted risk as embed cache). Must be added to bench/run.py _MANIFEST_ENV or it becomes an unrecorded score-adjacent flag. Concurrency: consolidation extracts from a thread pool, so the cache needs the embed cache's per-thread-connection WAL pattern. Expected impact is overstated: cross-run hits require a reused DATA_DIR (dominance_run.sh data/bench), but much of the repo's dev history uses a fresh data dir per experiment tag (data/bench_*_codex, run_dev_ablation per-role dirs) where a data_dir-scoped cache is cold; and salience/affect/contradiction calls remain uncached, so it is not near-100% of write tokens.

confirmed.title: score_importance is called and then fully overwritten by score_affect on the sleep path; product sleep() defaults the call ON despite documented zero ranking effect
confirmed.evidence: engine.py:1614-1616 fires one qwen-flash `score_importance` per pending record; when AFFECT_SALIENCE=1 the affect branch at engine.py:1646-1667 then sets `rec.importance = float(aff.get('importance', ...))` and recomputes `rec.salience` — score_affect always returns an 'importance' key (dashscope_client.py:598-603), so the first call's result is dead. Reproduced offline: with both enabled, counting client shows importance=1 call AND affect=1 call, final rec.importance=0.8 (affect) not 0.9 (score_importance). Separately, lifecycle.py:70 (`self.engine.consolidate_pending(scope=scope)`) — the sleep path MCP/API use (mcp_server.py:378,564) — inherits score_importance=True, even though the function's own docstring (engine.py:1381-1383) states it 'has no effect on retrieval accuracy', the bench adapter always passes False (bench/adapters/eidetic_adapter.py:162,165), and host auto-sleep already defaults it off (config.py:70, lifecycle.py:192-196).
confirmed.fix_sketch: Two surgical changes: (1) in consolidate_pending, skip the score_importance call for a record when the affect branch will score it (affect_salience_enabled, scorer callable, 'arousal' not in metadata), and in the affect except-handler fall back to score_importance if it was requested — final importance/salience provably identical in both success and failure paths (mock-client test asserts equal end state with one fewer call). (2) add SLEEP_SCORE_IMPORTANCE (default '1' = today's behavior) consumed by lifecycle.sleep, with the metabolism/product profile setting '0' to match the bench and auto-sleep paths. Both flag-safe, no integrity surface.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens/latency: one qwen-flash call (~1k input tokens) per pending record saved whenever affect is on, and per-record importance calls become opt-in on product sleep of bulk writes (a large haystack sleep currently pays N useless flash calls).

verdict.risk: Part (1) dedup must exactly reproduce three paths to stay behavior-identical: (a) affect success (dashscope score_affect always returns 'importance', so skipping is safe), (b) affect exception -> except-handler must call score_importance AND recompute salience with the 0.45/0.55 formula from engine.py:1616, preserving exception-propagation semantics (today a score_importance failure raises before the affect try-block; after the fix it raises inside the except handler), (c) a non-dashscope/mock client whose score_affect omits the 'importance' key currently falls back to the score_importance value via aff.get(..., rec.importance) — the dedup must call score_importance in that case or importance silently degrades to the pre-scoring value. Part (2): flag default '1' is behavior-neutral, but flipping the metabolism/product profile to '0' changes forgetting-side inputs — importance feeds FSRS initial stability (fsrs.py:51) and salience-threshold index pruning (retrieval.py:3341-3344), so metabolism deployments with SALIENCE_PRUNE_THRESHOLD>0 or FSRS-priority pruning could prune/decay records differently; the docstring's 'no retrieval accuracy effect' covers ranking under default config, not forgetting order. Mitigate by keeping the profile flip separate and A/B-able.

confirmed.title: Fixed 400-char window overlap re-sends ~11% of all haystack text per extractor and still splits turns mid-sentence
confirmed.evidence: chunk_text (dashscope_client.py:47-61) steps by chunk-overlap = 4000-400 = 3600 chars (config.py:436-437), so every window boundary re-transmits 400 chars; over a long haystack that is ~11% of the entire text, paid once per extractor (2x today per the edges+claims double call). The windows are cut at raw char offsets of the '\n'-joined 'role: content' session text (bench/adapters/eidetic_adapter.py:118-120, engine writes the same text), so a turn can still straddle a boundary with its head and tail in different windows whenever it is longer than the 400-char overlap — the exact failure the overlap exists to prevent.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Flag-gated (EXTRACT_ALIGNED_CHUNKS=0 default off) line-aligned chunker: pack whole '\n'-lines into <=extract_chunk_chars windows, carrying over only the last line (or zero lines) as overlap; fall back to the char chunker for any single line exceeding the cap. Pure and deterministic like chunk_text, so property tests can assert offline: (a) every input line appears verbatim in >=1 window, (b) no line is split across windows unless it alone exceeds the cap, (c) total chars submitted <= the char-chunker baseline. Capture fidelity is equal-or-better (fewer mid-fact splits), token cost strictly lower.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens: ~8-10% reduction of extraction input on long-session ingestion (stacks multiplicatively with the combined-call fix), with a small accuracy-side benefit from never splitting a turn mid-sentence.

verdict.risk: Three regression surfaces: (1) if the carried-over overlap line is not capped at the overlap budget, total submitted chars can EXCEED the char-chunker baseline (pathological long lines), inverting the token win — the implementation must carry zero lines when the last line is longer than the budget; (2) carrying only one short last line gives less cross-boundary context than today's guaranteed 400 chars, so multi-line facts (question on one line, elliptical answer on the next) straddling a window boundary may lose context the char chunker preserved — packing trailing whole lines up to the overlap budget mitigates this; (3) engine.py:1394-1404 _planned_extract_windows and bench/run.py:286 estimate window counts with the char chunker for the consolidation call budget — the aligned chunker must be mirrored behind the same flag or planned-vs-actual window counts diverge, skewing max_windows caps and telemetry. Also, long near-cap lines increase window COUNT (more calls, more fixed per-call system-prompt overhead and latency), though negligible for short conversational lines. Default path is byte-identical (flag off), so no default-behavior regression.

confirmed.title: recall_trace is unscoped: cross-namespace trace leak plus pooled-thread stale reads on the shared HTTP transport
confirmed.evidence: The MCP tool recall_trace (eidetic/mcp_server.py:581-587) and the HTTP route /api/recall_trace (eidetic/api.py:460-465) take NO namespace/agent/project arguments and return engine.recall_trace() verbatim. engine.recall_trace (eidetic/engine.py:2256-2265) first returns the calling thread's thread-local retriever.last_trace, else the single GLOBAL _last_completed_trace snapshot published after every ask (engine.py:1134-1138) with no scope filter. RecallTrace (eidetic/models.py:326-343) carries query text, parsed_query, scope, per-channel ranked memory ids, fused scores, and abstention_reason. The server docstring advertises shared multi-client HTTP deployment (mcp_server.py:21-22) and the instructions promise 'Reads never cross namespaces' (mcp_server.py:64-66). Additionally, retriever.last_trace is thread-local and never cleared after ask() (retrieval.py:3214-3223), and anyio.to_thread reuses pooled worker threads, so a standalone recall_trace call that lands on a worker that previously ran any client's ask() preferentially returns that stale foreign trace instead of the latest snapshot.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Scope the introspection surface on general grounds: (1) publish completed traces into a small bounded dict keyed by scope.key() instead of one global slot (same lock, e.g. last-N-per-scope=1); (2) add the standard namespace/agent_id/project_id parameters to the MCP tool and HTTP route, resolve via _scope(), and return only the trace whose trace.scope matches; (3) in the standalone cross-call surface, skip the thread-local fallback entirely (it is only correct for same-thread ask->prove splicing, which already reads it directly). No ranking or answer path is touched, so verify-or-abstain is unaffected.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity: restores the 'reads never cross namespaces' guarantee for the one tool that currently violates it, and makes recall_trace deterministic (no more scheduling-dependent stale traces) for the debugging/introspection story the product sells.

verdict.risk: Behavior change on the introspection surface: after scoping, a no-arg recall_trace call following an ask in a non-default namespace (no EIDETIC_NAMESPACE set) returns {} instead of the trace, breaking existing debug workflows; tests/test_brain_spine.py:227 (cross-thread no-arg fallback) and tests/test_mcp_server.py:210 must be updated. The per-scope snapshot dict must be genuinely bounded (LRU/last-1-per-scope) or it grows with namespace cardinality on long-lived shared servers. Removing the thread-local fallback also means a BRAIN_EVENTS-only configuration (trace flag off) can no longer surface a same-thread trace via engine.recall_trace() — consistent with the tool's documented '{} unless RECALL_TRACE' contract, but a subtle semantic shift for any future in-process caller.

confirmed.title: Self-repair loop dead-ends at the product surface: sync_health/memory_autopsy prescribe repairs no MCP tool or API route can execute
confirmed.evidence: sync_health returns "repair": "rebuild_index_from_store" when a derived surface is behind (eidetic/engine.py:915) and the MCP tool docstring advertises exactly that hint (mcp_server.py:370-371). memory_autopsy diagnoses vector_underfill with action 'repair/rebuild the vector index' (engine.py:2223-2225). But the engine methods that perform the repairs -- rebuild_index_from_store (engine.py:367), drain_reembed_queue (engine.py:497), apply_repair_proposals (engine.py:2238) -- are exposed on NEITHER transport: grep over mcp_server.py and api.py finds rebuild_index_from_store only inside the sync_health docstring, and the other two nowhere. An agent mounted via MCP can detect index debt but cannot heal it; the diagnosis tools hand back an instruction string that is unactionable through the plugin.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add one `repair` MCP tool + POST /api/repair with a dry_run=True default: dry-run returns the counted sync_health debt; apply=True runs rebuild_index_from_store (optionally then drain_reembed_queue) and returns before/after counted figures. Both operations only rebuild DERIVED caches from the immutable source of truth (the engine docstrings state this), so there is no verified-wrong risk and no raw deletion. Keep apply_repair_proposals gated behind its existing DREAM_REPAIR_APPLY flag if exposed at all.
confirmed.expected_impact: product: closes the detect->diagnose->repair loop that the tool descriptions already promise; a stale index (the one failure mode sync_health exists to catch) becomes fixable by the calling agent instead of requiring shell access to the host.

verdict.risk: Exposing apply=true on the unauthenticated HTTP API turns rebuild_index_from_store into a remote full-store re-embed trigger (embed-quota burn + long write-lock hold during the locked rebuild on large stores) — needs a flag gate or rate limit beyond dry_run default. Also a pre-existing concurrency window becomes reachable: records ingested between the off-lock snapshot/embed (engine.py:371-374) and the locked rebuild (engine.py:375-386) are added to the old index object that is replaced at engine.py:382, so the rebuilt index omits them and re-creates sync debt under concurrent ingest (self-detecting, re-runnable, but can chase its tail). Minor caveat on the finding itself: drain_reembed_queue IS indirectly reachable via the exposed sleep/consolidate tools (consolidate_pending calls it at engine.py:1387; lifecycle.py:83) — but that drains reembed debt only, not the vector/store count mismatch that sync_health's rebuild_index_from_store hint targets, so the core dead-end claim stands.

confirmed.title: HTTP /api/truth_ledger drops the as_of parameter, so bitemporal time travel on the proof-grade surface is MCP-only
confirmed.evidence: The route signature (eidetic/api.py:250-251) is (query, namespace, agent_id, project_id, verify) with no as_of, and the call at api.py:262 is engine().ask(query, verify=verify, scope=scope). The MCP twin accepts and forwards as_of (mcp_server.py:346-358), and the server instructions explicitly advertise '`recall`/`truth_ledger` accept `as_of`' (mcp_server.py:52-53). Every comparable HTTP route (AskIn, StructuredRecallIn, ReflexRecallIn at api.py:74-92) carries as_of; only the truth-ledger route lost it, so HTTP callers cannot get the supersession-chain ledger for a past moment even though engine.truth_ledger handles historical answers deterministically (engine.py:2280-2313).
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add as_of: Optional[float] = None to the route and pass it into engine().ask(..., as_of=as_of, ...) inside _answer_and_prove, exactly mirroring the MCP tool. One-line semantic change; the ledger enrichment already computes validity windows and is_current from the records, so no other code moves.
confirmed.expected_impact: product/accuracy: restores transport parity for the flagship 'time-travel with a proof chain' feature; HTTP integrators currently get silently present-time ledgers when they expected historical ones.

verdict.risk: Low. Adding an optional as_of query param to a GET route is backward compatible; it routes through the same verify-or-abstain engine.ask path MCP and /api/ask already use, so no new integrity surface. Two minor behavior changes: HTTP callers currently passing as_of (silently ignored today) will start receiving historical ledgers, and malformed as_of values become 422 validation errors instead of being dropped. Note the ledger's is_current field stays present-time-relative even in as_of mode (identical to the MCP twin) -- document that when landing so it is not misread as as-of-relative.

confirmed.title: Bulk import path (ingest_many) is unreachable from both transports: history imports cost N tool calls, N embed round trips, N write-lock acquisitions
confirmed.evidence: engine.ingest_many (eidetic/engine.py:430-488) is the designed bulk path: batched embeds ('N/10 round trips instead of N', engine.py:432-433) and all records written under ONE lock acquisition with one index save. Neither mcp_server.py nor api.py references it: the only write tools are single-item remember/remember_file (mcp_server.py:190-260) and /api/memories/text|file (api.py:150-183). Meanwhile the MCP instructions actively sell the import use case ('backdate imports with valid_at', mcp_server.py:57), so the advertised workflow runs through the slowest possible path: per-item embed call, per-item lock, per-item index save.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Expose a remember_many MCP tool + POST /api/memories/batch taking a bounded list (e.g. max 100 items) of {content, valid_at?, source?} validated with the existing _text_arg/_MAX_CONTENT_CHARS bounds, calling ingest_many and returning per-item _brief + pending_consolidation. Extend ingest_many to honor per-item valid_at (it already builds records per item; thread the value through instead of the single `va`). Write semantics (dedup, pending_consolidation, lossless substrate) are unchanged.
confirmed.expected_impact: latency/tokens: an M-fact import drops from M MCP round trips + M embedding calls to ceil(M/100) calls with batched embeds; also removes M-1 redundant index saves and lock acquisitions on the write path.

verdict.risk: Two real regression surfaces if the fix lands naively: (1) dedup divergence — ingest() dedups per-scope AND per-valid_at (engine.py:201-204; test_write_path.py:65-79 makes repeated events distinct for count/sum recall) while ingest_many() dedups per-scope only via get_by_hash (engine.py:444) with no intra-batch dedup, so a batch tool threading per-item valid_at would silently collapse distinct backdated repeat-events (or duplicate identical items within one batch) unless ingest_many's dedup is aligned to records_by_hash + per-valid_at with intra-batch seen-set. (2) orphan-blob invariant — ingest_many calls substrate.put (engine.py:448) BEFORE embed_texts (engine.py:452); a mid-batch embed failure leaves orphan raw blobs, violating the invariant ingest() deliberately preserves (engine.py:206-209, tested at test_write_path.py:45); public exposure requires put-after-embed reorder or failure cleanup. Also needs a batch-level payload bound (100 items x 1MB per-item _MAX_CONTENT_CHARS is 100MB worst case) and documented parity gaps (no MEMORY_INGESTED brain event, single lifecycle.after_ingest per batch, no memory-typing/flow-prime — all flag-off today).

confirmed.title: GET /api/memories returns the entire namespace with no pagination while the MCP twin is paginated
confirmed.evidence: The route (eidetic/api.py:278-283) does recs = engine().list_memories(scope) and returns [_record_brief(r) for r in recs] -- every record in the namespace, each with a 200-char snippet, full entity list, scope dict, and timestamps, in one response. The MCP list_memories tool on the same engine paginates with default 50 / max 500 and returns total/offset/limit (mcp_server.py:386-396). The HTTP surface therefore has an unbounded response: on a store of tens of thousands of memories this is a multi-megabyte JSON body and an O(n) serialization per call, and any UI poll repeats it.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Mirror the MCP contract: add limit (default 50, max 500 via the same bounds) and offset Query params, slice server-side, and return {total, offset, limit, memories}. Update the web UI fetch in eidetic/web to pass paging params. Pure read-surface change; no retrieval or verification logic involved.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens/latency: bounds the worst HTTP response from O(store size) to a fixed page; protects the shared server from accidental self-DoS by dashboards polling the list.

verdict.risk: Breaking response-shape change (list -> {total, offset, limit, memories}) for all HTTP consumers: web UI loadMemories at eidetic/web/index.html:549 iterates the raw array and tests/test_api_scope.py:88-91 assert list shape — both must be updated in the same change, plus any external scripts hitting the endpoint. Product risk: the web UI has no paging controls, so a default limit=50 silently truncates the memory list view for stores >50 records unless the UI adds paging/load-more in the same PR. Also note pagination only bounds the response body; engine.list_memories (engine.py:1917) still materializes and sorts the whole namespace per call, so server-side CPU stays O(n log n). Pagination bounds logic should be shared with mcp_server.py (_bounded_int, _DEFAULT_PAGE_LIMIT) rather than duplicated.

confirmed.title: recall(prove=True) ships every citation 2-3 times and silently ignores `limit` for the proof block
confirmed.evidence: mcp_server.py:279-281 truncates out["citations"] to `limit`, but line 284 builds the proof from the FULL Answer: prove_answer (eidetic/proofs.py:26-44) re-emits every citation as an evidence item including its full snippet, and proofs.py:46/54 copies whole contradiction items again into a third top-level list. So a prove=True response carries each cited snippet in `citations`, again in `proof.evidence`, and possibly again in `proof.contradictions`; and a caller who set limit=3 to bound tokens still receives all evidence items in the proof. /api/ask with prove=True (api.py:196-201) has the same duplication.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Two general-grounds trims: (1) when prove=True, drop the redundant top-level citations list (or emit citation refs only) since proof.evidence is a strict superset per item; (2) in prove_answer, make `contradictions` a list of memory_ids/indices into `evidence` instead of full copies. Keep evidence complete (do NOT truncate the proof itself -- grounded_count/provenance_complete must stay computed over all evidence, preserving verify-or-abstain semantics); raw text remains reachable via get_raw for any id.
confirmed.expected_impact: tokens: roughly halves the prove=True response weight per call (the dominant payload is snippets) without weakening the machine-checkable proof; makes the `limit` knob honest for callers budgeting context.

verdict.risk: Response-shape break for existing MCP recall(prove=True) and /api/ask prove=True consumers that read the top-level citations list (tool docstring promises 'answer plus cited immutable sources' — prefer the 'citation refs only' variant over outright dropping). Changing proof.contradictions to id refs breaks tests/test_flagships.py:26 (asserts contradictions[0]['memory_id'] dict shape) and the proofs.py docstring promise that the legacy proof tree is byte-identical; external auditors consuming contradiction items inline must join against evidence instead. No bench coupling: bench/ never calls prove=True, so zero benchmark-score risk; scripts/photographic_demo.py reads proof['evidence'] only and is unaffected.

confirmed.title: Count operator extracts calendar-year digits as the count, shipping verified-wrong answers like '2023' for 'how many times' questions
confirmed.evidence: _count_answer (record_ops.py:1037-1044) matches the FIRST number token in an atom with r"\b(one|two|...|\d+)\b" and returns it as the count. Offline probe (real RecordStore + claims_for_record + structured_recall): records 'User: I went to the dentist on 2023-11-15 for a cleaning.' / 'User: I went to the dentist again on 2023-11-20, filling this time.'; query 'How many times did I go to the dentist in November?' -> answered=True verified=True answer='2023' via _explicit_count_answer (record_ops.py:1446, action family 'go/went' hits, target 'dentist' hits, so the candidate is accepted). Reproduced with a second shape ('How many times did I visit the science museum?' over ISO-dated atoms -> '2023'). It ships because (a) the low-count fail-closed guard at record_ops.py:4345 only catches answers in {'one','two','1','2'}, and (b) count_aggregate is in _COMPUTED_OPS (verify.py:17), so verify.py:75 grants the anchor exemption and the verbatim source atom passes entailment with zero model calls -> verified=True on a nonsense answer. Masking probe: after _DATE_RE.sub on the atom, _count_answer returns '' and the downstream _generic_distinct_count_answer already computes the correct '2' from the same atoms.
confirmed.fix_sketch: In _count_answer, mask spans matched by the existing _DATE_RE, _RACE_TIME_RE, _CLOCK_TIME_RE, and _MONEY_RE (and standalone \b(19|20)\d{2}\b year tokens) out of the atom before scanning for the count number; return '' if nothing survives. Calendar tokens are never cardinalities of an action on general grounds. This is a 2-3 line edit inside an existing function (record_ops stays flat), and the probe shows _generic_distinct_count_answer then rescues the correct enumeration count '2' through the existing dispatch order, so the fix converts a verified-wrong channel directly into a verified-right one.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity: closes a verified-wrong channel on an extremely common shape (counting dated events - dates appear in most LoCoMo/LongMemEval session atoms); accuracy: distinct-count rescue turns these into correct structured answers instead of reader fallbacks.

verdict.risk: Rare legitimate counts get masked away: year-sized cardinalities (e.g. 'walked 2000 steps' under a count_aggregate plan) and count words inside money/clock spans now return '' from _count_answer, so those shapes fall from a structured answer to the reader LLM (token/latency cost, reader-dependent accuracy) — failure mode is abstain, never verified-wrong. Also the accuracy upside is partly overstated: for windowed shapes like 'in November' over ISO-dated atoms the pipeline abstains (no_structured_result) instead of being rescued by _generic_distinct_count_answer ('2' only rescues when atoms hit the target threshold, as in the museum shape), so bench gains depend on the reader fallback. Regression surface checked: all 156 SMQE tests (including fullpath/paraphrase/time/scope invariants and structured-recall surface) pass with the fix simulated via monkeypatch plugin.

confirmed.title: Superlative-over-group returns the temporal adjunct ('Wednesday') as the group label, and drops the label entirely for 'who' questions - verified-wrong via the witness exemption
confirmed.evidence: _extreme_label_from_atom's after-text fallback (record_ops.py:2173) matches r"\b(?:on|in|for|at)\s+([^.;!?]+)" after the winning measurement. Probe: records 'Maya ran 5 miles on Tuesday.' / 'Jonas ran 9 miles on Wednesday.' / 'Priya ran 3 miles on Thursday.'; query 'Which friend ran the most miles?' -> answered=True verified=True answer='Wednesday (9 miles)' (the weekday adjunct, not the friend). Query 'Who ran the most miles?' -> answer='9 miles' with no agent at all, because record_ops.py:2211 attaches a label only when the query contains 'which'. Both ship verified: _numeric_extreme_answer collects supports from all comparable groups (record_ops.py:2212-2221), the distinct memory_ids trigger the witness exemption (verify.py:65), and verbatim anchors pass without any model call.
confirmed.fix_sketch: General subject-labeling for group superlatives: when the query wh-head is 'who' or 'which <non-time noun>', extract the label from the SUBJECT preceding the verb in the winning atom (pattern on the stripped atom: ^((?:[A-Z][\w'-]+\s?){1,3})\s+\w+ before the measurement span), and reject after-text labels that are bare weekday/month tokens (reuse the existing _WEEKDAY_RE / month sets) unless the wh-head itself is a time word ('which day'). Extend the label-attachment condition at record_ops.py:2211 from 'which' to 'which|who|whose'. If no subject label is extractable for a who/which-agent question, fail closed rather than answer with the bare measurement. Small edits inside two existing functions; record_ops stays flat.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity: removes a verified-wrong channel ('Wednesday' as a friend); accuracy: group superlatives ('who ran the farthest', 'which hostel is cheapest' - the second already works) become structurally answerable with the correct group member as the answer.

verdict.risk: Subject-extraction regex can capture sentence-initial capitalized adverbials ('On Wednesday, Jonas ran 9 miles') or 'Last Tuesday' as pseudo-subjects unless the weekday/month rejection is applied to subject labels too, not only after-text labels — otherwise the fix trades one wrong label for another. Who-superlatives over first-person atoms ('I ran 9 miles') have no capitalized subject, so the fail-closed clause converts today's bare-measurement verified answers into abstentions (recall delta, integrity-correct direction). 'Which day/month did X do the most' questions must retain the after-text temporal label via the sketched time-word wh-head carve-out or they regress to abstention. Existing suite cases (tests/test_smqe.py:1930-1976) all derive labels from the before-text patterns and are unaffected. record_ops.py grows by a handful of lines inside two existing functions — flat in spirit but the implementer should offset if strict.

confirmed.title: Plural/list questions ship a single-record whole-atom answer as verified instead of enumerating equal-scored values across sessions
confirmed.evidence: _answer_value's unconditional catch-all `return _clean(text)` (record_ops.py:674) feeds the latest_value generic tail (record_ops.py:4642-4659). Probe: three records 'I visited Japan in March' / 'I visited Peru during the fall harvest' / 'Last week I visited Kenya for a safari' (all scored identically at 3.17 by _claim_atoms); query 'Which countries have I visited?' -> answered=True verified=True answer='Last week I visited Kenya for a safari' with ONE support. In the real verify path this passes the strict query-aware hypothesis (the record genuinely entails 'I visited Kenya'), so a 1-of-3 answer to an enumeration question ships as verified. There is no plural-enumeration operator: _process_list_answer is gated on the literal word 'process' (record_ops.py:4198) and _temporal_window_list_answer requires a temporal window.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add one general enumeration operator in qa_ops.py (keeping record_ops flat): when the query wh-head noun is PLURAL ('which/what <noun>s') with no superlative/count/temporal marker, run the same per-atom slot extraction across ALL atoms that meet the target-term threshold, collect distinct values (reuse _dedupe/_canonical_phrase), and answer the comma-joined list with one support per contributing record - distinct memory_ids make it verifiable under the existing witness rule. In record_ops, forbid the whole-atom _clean(text) fallback at line 674 from answering plural-head queries (a one-line guard, or delete the catch-all and let callers handle empty - a net shrink), so partial single-atom answers fail closed to the enumerator or reader instead of shipping as verified.
confirmed.expected_impact: accuracy: list-completeness questions (a standing LoCoMo multi-answer category) get full enumerations instead of verified 1-of-N answers; integrity: stops representing an incomplete list as a verified complete answer.

verdict.refutation: Core defect confirmed end-to-end (engine.structured_recall returns answered=True verified=True 'Last week I visited Kenya for a safari' with one support for 'Which countries have I visited?'), but two supporting claims are wrong. (1) 'No plural-enumeration operator' is false: _is_temporal_window_list_query (record_ops.py:1330-1352) has an explicit plural-target branch and returns True for this exact query, and _temporal_window_list_answer (1356) does NOT require a temporal window (line 1363 makes window filtering optional). It fires and returns empty only because the target-term threshold demands the hypernym 'country' in atom text and _action_object_phrase/_answer_value_specific extract nothing from these sentences — so the sketch would duplicate an existing operator instead of fixing its extraction gap. (2) 'Passes the strict query-aware hypothesis' is wrong for the shipped record backend: verify.py:104 gates the strict hypothesis on backend=='claim'; the record answer auto-entails via atom-substring-of-premise at verify.py:121 with no query-aware check at all. (3) The promised accuracy impact is unsubstantiated as sketched: 'the same per-atom slot extraction' provably returns '' on all three probe atoms, so the proposed enumerator would collect nothing — only the fail-closed guard half works, which is an integrity fix, not the promised full enumerations.
verdict.risk: Plural-head detection misfires on singular s-final nouns (the existing gate's own len>3+endswith('s') heuristic already treats 'class'/'bus'-type words as plural), turning previously correct verified answers into abstentions. Single-instance plural questions ('Which countries...?' when memory holds one visit) and 1-of-N answers that earn partial judge credit today fall through to reader fallback: token/latency cost and possible accuracy loss until the enumerator's extraction actually works (current extractors yield empty on simple 'I visited X' shapes). If the target-term threshold is relaxed to bridge the hypernym gap (country vs Kenya) so the enumerator can fire, category-mismatched values from target-matching atoms can ship inside a verified comma-joined list — per-witness entailment cannot check category membership. The 'delete the catch-all' variant of the fix would regress other _answer_value callers (scalar amount path at record_ops.py:4653); only the one-line plural guard is safe.

confirmed.title: Speaker-attribution edge cases: 'told <addressee> that X' returns the ADDRESSEE as the fact, and inverse 'who told me X' returns the content instead of the speaker
confirmed.evidence: Two confirmed probes. (1) _speaker_fact_value (record_ops.py:2762-2771) captures everything after the speech verb, then _clean (record_ops.py:211-216) truncates at the conjunction 'that': record 'User: I told Maya that the project deadline moved to Friday.'; query 'What did I tell Maya about the deadline?' -> answered=True answer='Maya' (the addressee, not the message; verified=True under the probe's substring verifier, and the real strict NLI hypothesis is the only remaining line of defense). (2) Inverse attribution: record 'Maya: The venue books up three months in advance.'; query 'Who told me the venue books up months in advance?' plans as latest_value and the generic tail answers 'The venue books up three months in advance' - the content, not 'Maya' - even though the role prefix holds the answer and _atom_role_terms (record_ops.py:2744) already knows how to read it.
confirmed.fix_sketch: (1) In _speaker_fact_value, skip the addressee NP after speech verbs before capturing: pattern (?:said|told|asked|mentioned...)(?:\s+(?:me|him|her|them|us)|\s+[A-Z][\w'-]+)?\s*(?:that\s+)?(.+) - a general dative-object rule, no entity lists. (2) In _speaker_fact_answer, when the query head is 'who' + speech verb ('who told/said/mentioned...'), return the speaker instead of the content: match topic terms against the atom body, then answer with the role-prefix name (the same m.group(1) _atom_role_terms already parses) with that atom as support. Both are contained edits to existing speaker functions; record_ops stays flat.
confirmed.expected_impact: accuracy: speaker-attribution questions (both directions - what-did-X-say works today, what-did-I-tell-Y and who-told-me-X do not) resolve structurally instead of shipping wrong-shaped answers or burning the structured attempt; integrity: removes an answered-wrong path that survives verification whenever the answer token appears verbatim in the premise.

verdict.risk: Fix 1 (dative-NP skip) as sketched is over-broad: applying the optional capitalized-NP skip to all speech verbs drops complement-clause subjects ("She said Tom's party was fun" -> "party was fun", currently correct); the skip must be restricted to ditransitive verbs (told/asked) or require a following 'that'. Fix 2 as sketched is unreachable for probe 2 — _speaker_fact_answer only runs when op=='speaker_fact' and planner.py:123 never routes 'who told me X' there (requires did/do/does/was/were between wh-word and verb) — so it needs a planner route change or a relaxation of _attribution_answer's atom-verb gate (record_ops.py:1271), each with its own regression surface: a who->speaker channel answering from role prefixes must keep the required-topic subset gate and exclude user/assistant roles, or it attributes any topically-matching utterance to whoever spoke it (and on the record backend that answer substring-verifies trivially per verify.py:121, so a bad attribution would ship verified=True). Fix 2 also adds a small branch to record_ops (marginal growth) unless implemented via the existing attribution machinery. Existing green tests around speaker_fact/_attribution could regress if the NP-skip changes captured spans for 'say/mention' atoms.

confirmed.title: No bridge-entity join: two-hop relative-clause questions ('Where does the person who adopted the beagle live?') always fail closed despite both hops existing as single atoms
confirmed.evidence: execute_plan (executor.py:11-21) runs exactly one single-shot operator pass; nothing in record_ops or qa_ops composes two atoms through a shared entity. Probe: records 'User: Priya adopted a beagle from the shelter last month.' / 'User: Priya lives in Denver with her sister.' / 'User: Marco lives in Austin.'; query 'Where does the person who adopted the beagle live?' -> answered=False failure_reason='no_structured_result'.
