Both hops are individually trivial for the existing machinery ('who adopted the beagle' -> Priya via subject extraction; 'where does Priya live' -> Denver via the existing _answer_value_specific 'where' branch at record_ops.py:597). The live-bench memory notes multi-hop as the known budget-starved recall gap on the reader path, which is exactly where these now land.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add one general two-hop operator in qa_ops.py (record_ops stays flat): detect the relative-clause shape 'the (person|one|friend|<noun>) who|that <pred>' in the query; resolve the bridge by scoring atoms against the <pred> terms and extracting the proper-noun SUBJECT of the best match (same subject-extraction pattern as the superlative-label fix); rewrite the query with the bridge entity substituted; re-run the normal single-hop pipeline (plan_query + _execute_atoms) on the rewritten query. Supports = the bridge atom plus the answer atom - distinct records, so the existing witness rule (verify.py:65) verifies the composition honestly, and no answer is produced unless BOTH hops anchor, preserving fail-closed behavior.
confirmed.expected_impact: accuracy + product: multi-hop join questions become structurally answerable with two-record verified citations instead of falling to the token-budget-starved reader; directly targets the identified multi-hop recall gap category.

verdict.risk: Main regression surface: wrong-bridge joins become verified=True answers — under the witness rule (verify.py:65) each hop only anchor-verifies verbatim against its own record, so the join itself gets no NLI check; if predicate-term scoring picks the wrong atom's subject (two people adopted pets, or incidental term overlap like a generic predicate 'the person who called'), the composed answer is confidently wrong. This intercepts relative-clause questions the reader fallback currently answers correctly. Mitigations must be part of the landing bar: fail closed on ambiguous bridges (require unique best subject and full predicate content-term coverage in the bridge atom), require the resolved entity to appear literally in the answer atom, add a depth-1 recursion guard on the query rewrite (nested relative clauses), and accept a small latency add on shape-matched queries. Also note two sketch overstatements: subject-position proper-noun extraction is new code (the existing 'who' machinery returns the whole sentence, and no 'superlative-label fix' is locatable in the codebase), and 'record_ops stays flat' holds only if the hook lives in executor.py/qa_ops via the existing _ro() re-entry (execute_claim_op/execute_record_op/_execute_atoms), not in the record_ops dispatch block.

confirmed.title: Ordinal reference into a recorded enumerated list ('the second hostel you recommended') fails closed even though the numbered list is stored verbatim
confirmed.evidence: Probe: record 'Assistant: Here are three hostels: 1. Cedar House - quiet garden. 2. Lantern Rest - near the station. 3. Mossgate Inn - rooftop bar.'; query 'What was the second hostel you recommended?' -> answered=False failure_reason='no_structured_result'. The only named-recall operator, _named_recommendation_answer, is gated on _REMIND_NAME_RE (qa_ops.py:209-210: 'remind me' / 'what was the name of'), which the ordinal phrasing does not match, and grep confirms no ordinal handling exists anywhere in record_ops.py, qa_ops.py, or retrieval.py. The answer is a deterministic index into an enumeration the store already holds ( _sentences at record_ops.py:196 even keeps numbered items as separate atoms with '2.' markers).
confirmed.fix_sketch: Generalize _named_recommendation_answer in qa_ops.py instead of adding a new pack: when the query contains an ordinal word (first|second|third|...|last) plus a head noun, look for atoms carrying enumeration markers (^\s*\d+[.)]) from a record whose text matches the head-noun terms, order the sibling atoms of that record by their marker value, and return the name of the Nth item via the existing _NAME_HEAD_RE. Ordinal words are closed-class function words - general grounds, no dataset coupling. Fail closed if the record has fewer than N enumerated items or the head noun does not match the list context.
confirmed.expected_impact: accuracy: ordinal coreference into previously recorded lists (a common follow-up shape after assistant recommendations) resolves structurally; tokens/latency: avoids shipping the whole list record to the reader for a one-token index lookup.

verdict.refutation: Core defect confirmed by offline probe (qa_ops.py:228 gate + no ordinal handler anywhere; even gate-matching phrasing fails since term-overlap needs >=2 hits and 'second' only appears in the _GENERIC_NAME_WORDS blocklist at qa_ops.py:219). But two overstatements: (1) the evidence claim about record_ops.py:196 _sentences is factually wrong — the splitter detaches 'N.' markers into standalone atoms ('2.' separate from 'Lantern Rest - near the station.') for both single-line and newline lists, so the sketch's atom-marker mechanism ('order sibling atoms by marker value') fails closed on the finder's own probe; the fix must parse the enumeration from the parent record's raw text instead. (2) accuracy impact is muted: no_structured_result falls back to retrieval+reader, which typically answers correctly from the surfaced record — the proven win is tokens/latency and structured coverage, not demonstrated end-to-end wrongness.
verdict.risk: All ordinal queries plan to op=latest_value (probed: 'first concert I went to', 'second job', 'second hostel you recommended'), so a generalized ordinal operator would also intercept temporal-ordinal questions; if a stored assistant list enumerates items matching the head noun ('Here are 3 concerts nearby: 1. ...'), it returns list item N as a grounded-but-wrong VERIFIED answer — NLI verification grounds the name but cannot validate index correctness, and unlike the existing operator there is no >=2 query-term-hits-in-answering-atom guard possible (the Nth item shares no terms with the query). Fix must additionally gate on recommendation framing in the query ('you recommended/suggested/listed'), require the head noun in the list's intro context, and fail closed when 2+ records contain qualifying enumerations (ambiguity between 'second item in your list' and 'second one recommended over time'). Without those gates this becomes the wave-E-style escape hatch the hard rules forbid.

confirmed.title: Record-backend SMQE answers bypass answer-level verification entirely: atom-substring check verifies the citation, never the answer
confirmed.evidence: verify.py:104-107 sets strict_hypothesis = (result.backend == "claim" and callable(retriever.verify)). For backend=="record" (everything produced by execute_record_op, record_ops.py:4669-4671 — the fallback for the whole 4671-line operator surface), verification falls to verify.py:121-124: if the proof atom is a whitespace/case-normalized substring of the premise -> ENTAILMENT 1.0; else verify_citation(rec, atom) — the ATOM is the hypothesis, never result.answer, never the query. Since record atoms are by construction sentences of the record text, the substring branch nearly always fires. Offline exploit (tiny synthetic store, fake NLI that refuses to entail the wrong answer): record "Alice adopted a cat named Miso in March.", StructuredAnswerResult(backend="record", answer="Alice adopted a dog.", proof_atom=<the cat sentence>) -> answer_from_result returns verified=True with 0 NLI calls. The docstring at verify.py:54-58 claims "Everything else must survive the strict query-aware hypothesis" — that only holds for the claim backend.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Drop the backend=="claim" gate in strict_hypothesis so record-backend results go through the same query-aware hypothesis (verify.py:115-117) with the same _atom_anchor_allowed exemptions for genuinely derived answers (computed ops, multi-witness composition). Cost stays bounded: anchored answers still take the free verbatim-anchor branch at verify.py:110-113; only non-anchored record answers gain a model check — exactly the ones currently self-verifying. No change to record_ops.py (stays flat).
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity: closes the largest verified-wrong channel — today any executor bug in any record op (wrong record selected, wrong slot extracted, wrong arithmetic) ships verified=True to MCP/API/bench via engine.structured_recall, and engine.ask post-answer reconsolidation then FSRS-reinforces the wrongly-confirmed citations. Verified-accuracy metrics currently overstate for the entire record backend.

verdict.risk: Record-backend SMQE answer yield will drop: single-support non-computed record results lose the free path entirely (the verbatim-anchor branch at verify.py:110 requires _atom_anchor_allowed, which is False for them — the finder overstates this as 'anchored answers still free'), so each takes a model NLI call with the query-aware hypothesis over raw-record premises (noisier than claim proof atoms), and any NLI refusal drops the whole answer (verify.py:139-140) into the full retrieval+reader fallback — latency and tokens rise, and accuracy can dip on questions where SMQE was right but the fallback is weaker. Residual gap: _COMPUTED_OPS stay anchor-exempt, so wrong-arithmetic-right-anchor executor bugs still self-verify (finder's 'closes wrong arithmetic' is overstated). Offline suite is blind to the change (test retrievers lack .verify, so they keep the lenient path; the only strict-retriever tests use backend='claim'), meaning regressions surface only in live benchmarks, which are quota-gated — ship behind a live A/B before promotion.

confirmed.title: _extractive_entailment proves short answers by boundary-free, question-free substring: 'Rome' entailed by 'syndrome', wrong year/count verified at 1.0
confirmed.evidence: retrieval.py:3102-3108: hn = _support_norm(answer); if len(hn) >= 4 and len(hn.split()) <= 12 and hn in pn -> ENTAILMENT 1.0. _support_norm (retrieval.py:2488) strips punctuation but the containment check is raw substring with no token boundaries and no question context. Offline exploits confirmed: _extractive_entailment("He was diagnosed with chronic fatigue syndrome.", "Rome") == True; ("She bought four apples at the market yesterday.", "four") == True; ("Back in 2019 she moved to Paris for a new job.", "2019") == True. This function is the first gate of verify_citation (retrieval.py:4453) and of _verify_candidates' batched path (retrieval.py:4530), where the hypothesis is the reader's answer alone — so a wrong short answer (wrong year, wrong count, wrong name) is verified=True whenever the string occurs anywhere in ANY retrieved candidate, and retrieved candidates are topically related so years/counts recur. The guard comment at 3104 ("Avoid proving tiny fragments like '3' or 'yes'") only blocks <4 chars; '2019', 'four', 'blue' all pass.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Two general tightenings: (1) match on token boundaries — compare hn's token sequence as a contiguous subsequence of pn.split(), killing the inside-word match; (2) for answers with <2 content tokens, do not accept bare containment: require the query-aware hypothesis (reuse _query_answer_hypothesis from smqe/verify.py) through model NLI, or an existing slot/temporal entailment helper that checks the premise actually binds the value to the asked-about slot. Multi-word verbatim quotes (the intended rescue) keep the fast path.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity + accuracy: eliminates verified-wrong on the most common benchmark answer shape (short factoids: years, counts, names, colors). Small latency cost only for 1-token answers that now go to NLI.

verdict.risk: Correct 1-token verbatim answers (the shape this fast path was built to rescue from flaky model NLI) may flip verified->abstained once routed through NLI, costing benchmark accuracy; one extra batched NLI call per short answer adds latency/cost; 'content token' definition must keep two-token value answers (e.g. 'blue 17', '5 years') on the fast path or tests/test_extractive_verification.py rescues regress. Boundary-tightening alone (token-subsequence match) is near-zero-risk; the sub-2-token NLI routing carries the accuracy trade.

confirmed.title: Claim-backend anchor exemptions (multi-record witness, option-choice, likely-inference, query-tie) verify only the cited atom — an answer contradicted by its own citation passes verified=True with zero model calls
confirmed.evidence: verify.py:109-120: when _atom_anchor_allowed is True and the atom is verbatim in the premise, ENTAILMENT 1.0 with no model call; the answer string is never compared to the atoms in any way. Exemptions are query-shape regexes: any 2 distinct support records auto-pass (verify.py:65-66); _OPTION_CHOICE_RE on the query (verify.py:77-78); _LIKELY_INFERENCE_RE (verify.py:81); same-record pairs with just 2 prefix-tolerant word overlaps with the query (verify.py:38-51, 72-73 — 'party'/'planning' ties via startswith). Four offline exploits confirmed, all verified=True with nli_calls=0: (a) event_order answer 'Bob visited Lisbon before Tokyo' when the two cited atoms date Tokyo BEFORE Lisbon; (b) query 'Would Alice prefer coffee or tea?' answered 'coffee' with sole cited atom 'Alice said she hates coffee and loves tea.'; (c) 'What breed would Dana likely adopt?' answered 'a chihuahua' citing 'Dana said she only wants large dogs.'; (d) same-record composition 'Sam signed the party invitations.' from atoms about lease papers and party planning.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Keep the exemption concept but add cheap answer-atom consistency checks before granting it: for option choices, the selected option token must appear in some support atom outside a negation window ('hates/never/not ... coffee' disqualifies); for multi-witness and query-tie compositions, require every content token of the answer to be covered by the union of atoms plus the query; for _COMPUTED_OPS, re-derive deterministically from the anchors (event_order: compare the anchor dates; temporal_delta/count: recompute) and only exempt when the re-derivation matches. All checks are lexical/arithmetic — no new model calls, no dataset knowledge.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity: today any executor misfire in composed/derived claim ops is invisible to verification; these checks convert silent verified-wrong into abstention while keeping legitimately derived answers zero-cost.

verdict.risk: Legitimate derived answers can newly lose the zero-cost exemption and fall back to strict query-aware NLI: event_order anchors without parseable inline dates, counts phrased as words ('twice' vs '2'), option answers with benign nearby negation ('not bad, she loves coffee'), paraphrased composition tokens absent from atoms. Those cases regress from free-verified to extra NLI calls (latency/tokens) and possibly abstention (accuracy drop on temporal/multi-hop/preference slices) — the exact cases the wave-G witness rule and exemptions were added to rescue. Verify-side deterministic re-derivation (date compare, count recompute) duplicates executor semantics in verify.py; drift between the two implementations silently denies exemptions. Fix must gate _atom_anchor_allowed itself, not just the verbatim shortcut at verify.py:110, or the bare-atom NLI retry at :119-120 re-opens the hole with model calls; and the coverage union must be atoms-only, not atoms+query, or question-echo compositions (exploit d) still pass. Existing tests covering the exemptions will need updating.

confirmed.title: _aggregation_proof_support clause-borrow sums unrelated amounts: wrong inflated total gets deterministic ENTAILMENT 1.0 while the correct total is rejected
confirmed.evidence: retrieval.py:1780-1789 (_relevant_amount_values): for every clause with an amount, evidence = previous clause + clause unconditionally — the comment says the look-back is for anaphors ('It was $800') but the code never checks the clause IS anaphoric, so a clause with its own distinct subject borrows the scope term. Offline exploit (AGGREGATION_AUDIT=1): record 'Alice spent $30 on books. Her rent was $900.', query 'How much did Alice spend on books in total?' -> source_values=[30, 900], total=930; wrong answer '$930' matches and _aggregation_proof_support (retrieval.py:4475-4511) returns support, which _verify_candidates (4568-4581) upgrades to ENTAILMENT/1.0 -> verified=True. The correct answer '$30' is REJECTED by the same proof (sum mismatch). Also the upgrade loop at 4571-4577 overwrites any prior label including CONTRADICTION. Flag defaults off (config.py:538) — this is a pre-flip finding.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Gate the look-back on the amount clause actually being anaphoric: previous-clause borrowing only when the clause has a pronominal/empty subject (starts with 'it/that/this/which was' or has no scope-bearing noun of its own); additionally, exclude amounts whose own clause carries a conflicting scope noun (a term set from the clause disjoint from the query terms). Never upgrade a citation whose prior NLI label is CONTRADICTION.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity: prevents the deterministic-proof channel from certifying reader double-count/over-sum errors at confidence 1.0 (and from punishing the correct total) if AGGREGATION_AUDIT ships; keeps genuine compositional-sum proofs intact.

verdict.risk: The fix narrows the deterministic aggregation proof: genuine compositional sums whose item clauses lack query-term overlap ("She spent $20 on novels" for a "books" query) or have non-pronominal referring subjects ("The total came to $45") lose the ENTAILMENT/1.0 upgrade and fall back to single-source NLI, which cannot entail compositional arithmetic — so correct aggregate answers abstain/go unverified more often under AGGREGATION_AUDIT (accuracy/verified-rate regression on money-total questions, but fail-closed so integrity is preserved). The anaphor gate itself is a regression surface: a naive starts-with-pronoun check would still let 'Her rent was $900' borrow — it must test for a pronominal HEAD subject or absence of a distinct scope-bearing noun. The CONTRADICTION-never-upgrade rule is low risk (conservative only). Note one overstatement in the finding: in the simple single-source case the correct answer '$30' is not punished — it still verifies via ordinary extractive/NLI entailment; the correct-answer-rejected inversion only occurs in genuinely compositional cases. Exposure is higher than stated: METABOLISM_PROFILE (config.py:44) sets AGGREGATION_AUDIT=1 under METABOLISM_MODE, the shipping bench profile, so this is live there, not merely pre-flip.

confirmed.title: Advice sentence rescue: contradiction kill-switch only scans the first 5 long sentences, and the whole multi-sentence advice answer ships verified=True on one entailed restatement
confirmed.evidence: retrieval.py:4706-4721: sentence_claims = [cl for cl in _sentences(text) if len(cl) >= span_nli_min_chars][:5] — sentences beyond the 5th, and any sentence under 12 chars (config.py:428), are never contradiction-checked, though the block's own comment promises 'any CONTRADICTION kills the rescue'. On rescue, citations/entailed are replaced by the single restatement sentence's results (4720), verified=(entailed>0) at 4768, and 4811-4812 filters citations to that restatement — so the surfaced proof covers none of the actual advice content. NEUTRAL (fabricated-but-uncontradicted) recommendation sentences ride under verified=True with note 'verified: advice grounded on context restatement' (4800-4801). Concrete failure: a 6-sentence advice reply whose 6th sentence contradicts a stored allergy/constraint memory is rescued and returned verified=True. Also each sentence's entailment goes through _extractive_entailment first, so any verbatim ≤12-word restatement triggers the rescue with no model call.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Run the contradiction scan over ALL answer sentences (with batch_nli_enabled this is one batched call; keep the [:5] cap only for the search for an entailed restatement). Surface the non-entailed sentences in unverified_claims instead of leaving it empty, so the verified flag is auditable, and require the rescuing restatement citation set to be reported alongside a distinct note rather than replacing the full citation list.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity + product: closes the escape where memory-contradicting advice ships verified after sentence 5, and makes the 'verified advice' surface honest about which part was actually proven.

verdict.risk: Scanning all sentences multiplies NLI pairs (sentences x candidates) on every non-entailed advice answer in the default product path -- latency/token cost, and per-sentence batching as sketched is N batched calls, not one, without an extra sentence-x-candidate batching refactor. Noisy CONTRADICTION labels on novel suggestion sentences will kill more rescues, demoting more advice answers to unverified/abstain (deliverability drop on advice queries -- intended tradeoff but measurable). Populating unverified_claims while verified=True changes the proofs packet shape (proofs.py:55); only proofs.py reads it today, low risk. Existing pin test tests/test_abstention.py:72 stays green.

confirmed.title: SMQE record-op scan re-tokenizes every atom per (atom x target) with zero memoization -- seconds of pure CPU per question
confirmed.evidence: engine.ask() runs SMQE structured_answer unconditionally on every question (eidetic/engine.py:964-967), and execute_plan scans ALL active records (eidetic/smqe/engine.py:114). Measured on 600 records x 8KB text: execute_plan takes 0.7-3.8 SECONDS per question of pure Python (latest_value 3.0-3.8s, relative_temporal 2.1s) before any model call. cProfile shows _terms (record_ops.py:61) called 241,866 times, _expanded_terms (record_ops.py:1110) 169,263 times, _count_term_key (record_ops.py:1122) 4.1M times for ONE question -- _latest_atom_target_hit (record_ops.py:2645) calls _expanded_terms(atom) fresh for every atom/target pair, re-running the same regex tokenization on identical strings ~10x each. _record_atoms (record_ops.py:427) re-splits every immutable record into atoms on every ask (2.5s cumulative in profile).
confirmed.fix_sketch: Memoize the pure text->terms functions: functools.lru_cache (str -> frozenset) on _terms and _expanded_terms, and cache _record_atoms keyed by the record's content_hash (record text is immutable by design, so the cache can never go stale). These are pure functions -- outputs are byte-identical, so verify-or-abstain semantics are untouched, and record_ops.py shrinks or stays flat (decorators; the duplicated plural-stemming logic in _terms/_count_term_key can be unified into one cached helper).
confirmed.expected_impact: latency: ~5-10x reduction of the dominant per-question CPU (profile shows ~10x call duplication on _terms); saves roughly 0.5-3s per question at moderate corpus sizes on EVERY ask, product and benchmark alike, growing linearly with corpus size.

verdict.risk: Three implementation hazards, all avoidable: (1) The sketch's '_record_atoms cache keyed by content_hash' must NOT be implemented literally — content_hash defaults to '' (models.py:99), so hashless records would collide and atoms would be mis-attributed to the wrong record, yielding wrong citations that can still pass atom-level verification (a verified-wrong-citation channel); also _record_atoms falls back to rec.summary (record_ops.py:432), which consolidation can change under a constant content_hash (staleness). Key the sentence-split cache by the input string itself (lru_cache on _sentences(text)) — that is the variant the A/B probe measured, byte-identical. (2) lru_cache returning shared set objects: audited all ~60 call sites of _terms/_expanded_terms — none mutate the result today, but a future caller calling .add()/.update() on a cached set would silently corrupt every subsequent lookup; return frozenset as the sketch proposes (all current usages are frozenset-compatible: &, -, <=, iteration, update-into-other-sets). (3) Unbounded caches grow with total ingested text on a long-running MCP server (~tens of MB per few-MB corpus) — use bounded maxsize. _terms reads module-level _STOP/stemming tables at call time; verified these are load-time constants never mutated, so no staleness today, but any future runtime-configurable stopword feature would need cache invalidation. Note the impact claim is scale-dependent: cost is linear in corpus text (measured 16KiB→17ms, 108KiB→92ms, 1.6MiB→1.4s), so 'seconds saved per ask' holds at multi-MB product corpora while bench-conversation-scale namespaces save tens-to-hundreds of ms.

confirmed.title: Default verify path issues up to final_topk serial NLI network calls per answer; shipped batch NLI is flag-off
confirmed.evidence: _verify_candidates' default branch (retrieval.py:4551-4553, taken because BATCH_NLI defaults to 0 at config.py:574 and FAST_VERIFY to 0 at config.py:579) loops serially over all candidates (final_topk=10 plus audit-appended extras) calling verify_citation -> client.nli, a full governed chat round-trip each when local extractive proof fails. The advice rescue (retrieval.py:4706-4718, not flag-gated) re-runs _verify_candidates for up to 5 sentences, each another serial sweep. At DASHSCOPE_RPM=60 (config.py:219) a question consuming ~12 governed calls caps benchmark throughput near 5 questions/min and adds multiple seconds of serial round-trips per answer.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Make the batched path the default after a parity audit (nli_batch already keeps per-pair entailment-only semantics and treats unjudged pairs as neutral -- conservative, never verified-wrong), or parallelize the per-candidate verify_citation calls across the governor's 4 concurrency slots with results merged positionally. Either is general-grounds: same premises, same hypothesis, same labels; a missing judgment stays NEUTRAL so no verified-wrong channel can open.
confirmed.expected_impact: latency + tokens: N serial NLI round-trips collapse to 1-2 requests per answer; per-answer verification drops from ~5-20s to ~1-2s and RPM-limited benchmark throughput improves ~3-4x.

verdict.risk: Flipping BATCH_NLI default breaks the documented byte-identical-baseline guarantee (retrieval.py:4516-4518): batch truncates premises to 3000 chars vs 6000 in single-call nli, so long-premise entailments can flip to neutral (more abstentions, accuracy dip on 3-12k-char sources); one call judging 12 pairs risks cross-pair premise leakage mislabeling which citation entails, polluting attribution-driven signals (reconsolidation, verified-helpful) even though answer-level verification stays sound; measured 2.3x speedup (RESULTS_locomo_n40.md:46) conflated BATCH_NLI with FAST_VERIFY so batch-only accuracy attribution is unmeasured. The alternative parallel-verify option is semantics-preserving but only cuts wall-clock latency (RPM/tokens unchanged) and threads concurrency through verify_citation's image tempfile path. Advice-rescue early-stop-on-contradiction ordering (retrieval.py:4711-4716) must be preserved under any batching of the rescue loop.

confirmed.title: Per-verified-answer reconsolidation re-embeds unchanged text (a cache-hit no-op) then rewrites the entire index file under the global write lock
confirmed.evidence: DEFER_REEMBED defaults to 0 (config.py:577), so every confirmed citation triggers embed_text(rec.text) inline (engine.py:1058-1064). Record text is immutable and EMBED_CACHE defaults on (config.py:209), so the persistent cache keyed by (model, dim, sha256(text)) returns the IDENTICAL vector indexed at ingest -- index.update writes the same vector, yet reembed is non-empty so engine.py:1095-1096 calls index.save() under self._write_lock: a whole-file atomic rewrite measured at ~22MB / 11-20ms at 5k records (numpy and HNSW backends both), growing linearly, on every verified answer -- including pure-local SMQE answers that otherwise made zero model calls. With a cold embed cache it is worse: one real embed network call per confirmed citation per answer.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Skip the re-embed when the text is unchanged since last index write -- the embed-cache key already proves identity, or simply flip DEFER_REEMBED default to 1 (the drain path drain_reembed_queue already exists, embeds off-lock, and is idempotent) and only call index.save() when an applied vector actually differs. Identical vectors in, identical vectors out: zero accuracy or integrity change, the write lock is held only for the sqlite FSRS upserts.
confirmed.expected_impact: latency: removes ~11-20ms (linearly growing) of lock-held disk IO per verified answer plus a redundant cache lookup per citation, eliminating writer serialization stalls between concurrent asks/ingests; tokens: eliminates per-answer embed calls whenever the cache is cold.

verdict.risk: Two variants with different risk. (a) Vector-diff variant (skip index.update/save when the re-embedded vector equals the indexed one): near-zero risk, but the comparison must be post-normalization (index.update normalizes, embed_text returns raw) or the check never matches and the fix is silently inert; tests asserting a save per verified answer would need updating. (b) DEFER_REEMBED default flip: changes baseline behavior (repo discipline is flag-off defaults), and the reembed queue is in-memory only, drained only on consolidate_pending/sleep — a restart drops it, so a genuine post-model-migration vector refresh could be delayed indefinitely instead of landing at answer time. Durability is not a real regression: INDEX_SAVE_DEBOUNCE defaults to 1 (every ingest saves), and the index is a documented derived cache with rebuild_index_from_store recovery. No accuracy/integrity surface: the reconsolidation block runs after the answer is finalized, and identical vectors mean identical retrieval either way.

confirmed.title: INDEX_SAVE_DEBOUNCE=1 default rewrites the whole vector index file on every single-item ingest -- O(N^2) cumulative IO under the write lock
confirmed.evidence: _maybe_save_index (engine.py:351-358) with index_save_debounce default 1 (config.py:583) calls index.save() inside the write lock on every ingest(). Both backends serialize the FULL index (vector_index.py:172-180 numpy npz; 338-351 HNSW bin+meta+struct). Measured: at 5k records one save writes ~22MB in 11-20ms; ingesting a 5k corpus one item at a time (the MCP memory_write product path) therefore writes ~55GB cumulative disk IO and holds the global write lock for the save each time, blocking concurrent asks' reconsolidation phase. NumpyVectorIndex.add additionally does a full-matrix np.vstack copy per add (0.77ms avg at 5k, also quadratic cumulative).
confirmed.fix_sketch: Raise the default debounce (e.g. 32-64) and pair it with the existing durability backstops: flush_index() on shutdown, the idle/sleep cadence, and rebuild_index_from_store() which the code already documents as the recovery path for a lost derived index (the sqlite+substrate remain the fsynced source of truth, so no data-loss window for raw memory). Optionally add a time-based flush (save if >T seconds since last).
confirmed.expected_impact: latency: single-item write-path cost drops by the full save time (11-20ms at 5k, linear growth) and cumulative ingest IO drops from O(N^2) to O(N); write-lock hold times shrink so concurrent read/write throughput improves.

verdict.risk: Crash/kill durability window widens from ~0 to debounce-1 records: sqlite has records the on-disk vector index lacks, causing silent recall misses (and possibly a stale-but-verified answer to 'latest value' asks) until rebuild_index_from_store re-embeds the whole store at full embed-API cost. The fix sketch's claimed backstops are partly missing today: flush_index() is defined but wired to no shutdown path, and the idle/sleep cadence (lifecycle.py) never saves the index — the wiring must be ADDED, not just 'paired with'. Also the win is partial as sketched: PERSISTENT_BM25 defaults ON, so engine.py:300 still rewrites the full BM25 JSON per single ingest unless the debounce also gates save_lexical. Finally, raising the default changes baseline behavior, contra the repo's flags-default-off discipline — legal under the stated hard rules but a deliberate call.

confirmed.title: Governor burns an RPM token before the concurrency-slot wait and turns slot timeout into a non-retried hard failure
confirmed.evidence: _slot() (dashscope_client.py:218-228) calls _take_token() FIRST, then sem.acquire(timeout=30s default, config.py:224). A thread that then waits behind the 4-slot semaphore (config.py:220) has already consumed an RPM token it may never use; on timeout it raises ModelCallError. In run() (dashscope_client.py:238-241) that raise happens at `with self._slot():` -- OUTSIDE the try block -- so it propagates immediately with no retry/backoff and the token is never re-credited. Under a burst of >4 concurrent long calls (reader generations of 30-60s), every waiting call both starves the shared RPM bucket and hard-fails the whole question after 30s; this matches the 'governor exhaustion' failure taxonomy already observed in the wave-E report (commit 0ad1ecb).
confirmed.fix_sketch: Two general-grounds changes: (1) re-credit the token bucket (tokens += 1 under the lock) when the semaphore acquire times out, so queued-but-never-dispatched calls stop consuming RPM; (2) treat slot-timeout as a retryable condition inside run() -- catch it, back off, and retry bounded by max_retries -- instead of failing the question on first timeout. Neither changes any model-call semantics; a call still either really runs or fails loud after bounded retries.
confirmed.expected_impact: latency + product: under concurrency bursts, questions queue instead of hard-failing at 30s, and effective throughput tracks the configured RPM instead of being silently eroded by tokens burned during semaphore waits.

verdict.refutation: Survives, with one overstatement: the RPM-bucket-starvation half is minor at default config (rpm=60 refills 1 token/sec while the 4-slot semaphore caps long-call throughput at ~4-8 calls/min, so the semaphore binds long before the bucket). The dominant, well-evidenced defect is the non-retried hard failure at slot timeout — confirmed at dashscope_client.py:219-228 (token taken before acquire, no re-credit on timeout) and :238-241 (raise inside _slot() __enter__ occurs before the try, bypassing all retry/backoff), and matched by the wave-E report's 4 rows dead on 'concurrency slot unavailable after 240s'.
verdict.risk: (1) tests/test_rate_governor.py:139 pins fail-loud-fast slot timeout with max_retries=0; the fix must bound slot-timeout retries by max_retries or that test breaks. (2) Under true saturation, time-to-failure grows from one slot timeout (30s default) to up to (max_retries+1) timeouts plus backoff (~3-4 min at defaults), so failures may surface as slower wall-clock-deadline aborts upstream and bench runs fail slower. (3) Token re-credit must be capped at bucket capacity to avoid inflation. (4) Must preserve token-then-slot acquisition order (documented deadlock rationale); the sketch does.

confirmed.title: Every ask() pays two full-corpus record loads plus a full active-claims load, all pydantic-parsed row by row
confirmed.evidence: structured_recall loads active_records_at AND active_claims_at for the whole scope before even computing the plan (smqe/engine.py:78-86); retrieve() then loads the identical corpus AGAIN (retrieval.py:3338). store.active_claims_at (store.py:378-396) parses every row via ClaimRecord.model_validate_json. Measured at 6,000 claims + 1,500 records: 20.1ms per active_claims_at call (15.5ms of it pydantic parsing, 4.6ms SQL) + 6.7ms per record load, i.e. ~30-35ms of pure deserialization per question, twice for records, growing linearly with namespace size -- paid even by questions whose plan op never consumes claims.
confirmed.fix_sketch: The engine already maintains a monotonic per-namespace version bumped under the write lock on every content write (engine.py:159-163). Add a version-keyed snapshot cache: (namespace, ns_version, as_of-bucket) -> (active_records, active_claims), invalidated automatically when the version bumps; and thread the SMQE-loaded corpus into the subsequent retrieve() call within the same ask so the corpus is deserialized once per question. Additionally compute plan_query first and skip the claims load for ops execute_claim_op cannot use. Pure caching of reads keyed on the existing write-versioning -- no truth-staleness channel (same mechanism the answer cache already trusts).
confirmed.expected_impact: latency: saves ~30-60ms per question at current benchmark scale and caps the O(n)-per-question growth (at 20-30k claims this alone becomes 100ms+ per ask); read-mostly workloads amortize to near-zero.

verdict.refutation: Minor overstatement only: not "every ask()" pays two record loads — exact-hash cache hits pay zero and SMQE-answered asks pay one; the double record load applies to the retrieval-fallback path (SMQE abstains), which is the common case. Also the "skip claims for ops execute_claim_op cannot use" sub-fix is near-worthless: _execute_atoms (record_ops.py:4248) tries its helper chain regardless of plan.op, so almost every plan consumes claim atoms. Core claim stands: offline probe at 1,500 records/6,000 claims reproduced the timings (active_claims_at 19.8ms with 14.8ms pydantic parse; active_records_at 5.9ms; ~32ms deserialization per fallback question, O(n) growth), and the double load is real (smqe/engine.py:78-85 then retrieval.py:3339).
verdict.risk: (1) Stale ranking-side fields: FSRS reinforce/verified-helpful writes on every answered ask, consolidate() summaries, and set_metadata deliberately do NOT bump ns_version (engine.py:160-162), so a cross-ask snapshot freezes salience/fsrs/summary values that feed ranking (retrieval.py:3344, 3913) — cached vs uncached asks stop being byte-identical; must ship behind a default-off flag mirroring cache_versioning_enabled. (2) Records/claims with future valid_at/invalid_at can change active-status by time passage alone with no version bump — snapshot must bypass or exact-key as_of/time-travel queries (the answer cache already bypasses as_of). (3) Implementation ordering is load-bearing: version must be read BEFORE the corpus load (bump happens after upsert under the lock), else a race caches pre-write corpus under a post-write version = stale-truth channel. (4) Cached MemoryRecord objects are shared across threads; read paths currently never mutate them (verified by grep) but that becomes an unguarded invariant. (5) Memory footprint: full corpus + claims per namespace is tens of MB at 20-30k records; needs an LRU/cap for multi-namespace servers. Verify-or-abstain integrity is preserved: truth content (text/validity) only changes via bumping paths (store.invalidate_record has no in-package callers) and SMQE verification re-reads records fresh via store.get_record (smqe/verify.py:92).

confirmed.title: Run manifest omits ~40 score-affecting env vars (model IDs, abstention weights, cascade flags) despite claiming to be exhaustive
confirmed.evidence: bench/run.py:24-26 states 'Every score-affecting env flag is recorded so a claim reproduces from its manifest alone... deliberately exhaustive; an unrecorded flag is a reproducibility hole'. Diffing _MANIFEST_ENV against every env var parsed in eidetic/config.py shows these wired, score-affecting flags are NOT recorded: model identities TEXT_EMBED_MODEL/EMBED_DIM (dashscope_client.py:458,513 — the dense channel), VERIFY_MODEL (dashscope_client.py:712-781 — the NLI verify-or-abstain gate), EXTRACT_MODEL (dashscope_client.py:641), GEN_MODEL, RERANK_MODEL, SALIENCE_MODEL, CONSOLIDATE_MODEL; the four ABSTENTION_W_ENTAIL/COVERAGE/AGREEMENT/PROOF weights (retrieval.py:4599-4600 — ABSTENTION_V2 and ABSTENTION_V2_TAU are recorded but the weights that compose the confidence are not); SPECULATIVE_CASCADE (retrieval.py:4682) and DIFFICULTY_ADAPTIVE_DEPTH (retrieval.py:4048); CACHE_COSINE; REFLEX_RECALL_MIN_COVERAGE and all REFLEX_W_*; RRF_W_ACTIVE_FACT and ACTIVE_FACT_CONTEXT_TOPK; the evidence-audit depths TEMPORAL_EVIDENCE_TOPK/LIST_EVIDENCE_TOPK/GRAPH_BRIDGE_TOPK/USER_EVIDENCE_TOPK/ASSISTANT_EVIDENCE_TOPK (while RAW_SPAN_AUDIT_TOPK IS recorded — inconsistent); TYPE_PRIOR_WEIGHT, LAMBDA_SALIENCE/AFFECT_W_*/SALIENCE_GAMMA, ROCCHIO_ALPHA/BETA/TOPR/CONF_GATE, SCRATCHPAD_TOPK/MIN_SALIENCE/CHANNEL_WEIGHT, SPAN_NLI_MIN_CHARS, COVE_QUESTIONS. Additionally INGEST_WINDOW_TURNS is read by bench/adapters/eidetic_adapter.py:141 (ingest windowing) but absent from the manifest, while its sibling INGEST_GRANULARITY is recorded.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Make config.py the single source of truth: have _get/_get_bool/_get_int record each env name into a module-level ENV_REGISTRY as Settings fields are constructed, export it, and build _MANIFEST_ENV as ENV_REGISTRY minus an explicit documented secrets/infra allowlist (DASHSCOPE_API_KEY, OSS_*, ADBPG_DSN, GDB_ENDPOINT) unioned with the bench-only reader/judge/adapter keys. Add a hygiene test that scans eidetic/config.py and the bench score-path modules (reader.py, judge.py, adapters/*) for env-literal reads and fails when any name is neither in _MANIFEST_ENV nor the allowlist. This auto-extends the manifest whenever a flag is added, exactly like the existing METABOLISM_PROFILE union already does for profile keys.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity: today two runs with byte-identical manifests can differ on the verify-or-abstain model, the embedding model, or the abstention weights — the exact knobs ablation claims attribute wins to. Closing this makes every future scoreboard claim actually reproducible from its manifest, as the program already promises.

verdict.risk: Manifest env dict roughly doubles (133 -> ~230 keys), so manifests written before/after the change diff loudly and any downstream tooling that eyeballs env diffs sees churn (merge_artifacts uses a separate hand-picked _ENV_CONTRACT_KEYS tuple and gate.py reads only READER_MODEL, so neither breaks). The auto-registry must force a Settings() construction before manifest build (field default_factory closures register lazily) and must union in bench-only keys (READER_*/JUDGE_*/BENCH_*) not read via config._get, or the new list silently drops currently-recorded keys. Biggest real hazard: a wrong secrets allowlist could serialize a credential (DASHSCOPE_API_KEY, OSS_*, ADBPG_DSN) into artifacts; the hygiene scan test also needs an infra/timeout allowlist and will need occasional maintenance to avoid false failures.

confirmed.title: GUARD_ENABLED / GUARD_MIN_DELTA_PP / GUARD_ALPHA are dead env vars, yet docs state the promotion rule in terms of them
confirmed.evidence: config.py:396-398 defines guard_enabled/guard_min_delta_pp/guard_alpha ('a tuned config is promoted only if it beats the champion on the DEV split by >= min_delta_pp AND a paired McNemar test is significant'). Nothing reads these fields or env vars: bench/guard.py takes min_delta_pp/alpha solely from argparse defaults (bench/guard.py:206-207, `--min-delta-pp` default 1.0, `--alpha` default 0.05) and never imports Settings. Meanwhile bench/DOMINANCE_PROGRESS.md:925 states the promotion rule as 'a flag becomes a default ONLY after GUARD_ENABLED=1 GUARD_MIN_DELTA_PP=1.0 ...' and docs/self-healing.md:62 documents the same env semantics — an operator exporting GUARD_MIN_DELTA_PP=2.0 to tighten promotion gets a silent no-op and promotions continue at +1.0pp.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Either delete the three fields from Settings (config shrinks) and rewrite the two doc sentences in CLI-arg terms, or make guard.py main() default its argparse values from get_settings().guard_min_delta_pp/guard_alpha and refuse to run when guard_enabled is false. Add a unit test that sets the env vars and asserts guard_decision's effective thresholds change (or that the fields no longer exist). General-grounds only; no benchmark coupling.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity: the auto-revert guard is the promotion discipline the whole flip-table program leans on; env-documented thresholds that are silently ignored mean the discipline can be believed tightened when it is not.

verdict.risk: Option B (wire Settings into guard.py + refuse when guard_enabled=0) would silently disable the existing promotion hook: scripts/dominance_run.sh:93 runs bench.guard with `|| true` and GUARD_ENABLED defaults to 0, so promotions would quietly stop until operators export GUARD_ENABLED=1; eidetic/optim/daemon.py:62 guard_command has the same exposure. Sourcing argparse defaults from get_settings() also lets stray GUARD_* env in CI shift guard behavior. Option A (delete fields) is near-zero risk but must also fix .env.example:109-111 (a third doc surface the finding missed) alongside DOMINANCE_PROGRESS.md:925 and docs/self-healing.md:62. Severity nuance: current argparse defaults (1.0/0.05) coincide with documented env defaults, so no divergence exists today unless an operator changes the env — latent operator trap in offline tooling, not live answer-integrity.

confirmed.title: Seven documented config knobs are read nowhere: CASCADE_CONFIDENCE, ANOMALY_THRESHOLD, MEMORY_MANAGER_DUP_COSINE, DREAM_RULE_MAX_LEN, FLOW_SESSION_DECAY, SALIENCE_ALPHA, CONSOLIDATION_INTERVAL_SEC
confirmed.evidence: Token-level scan of the repo (field name + env name, including getattr forms) confirms zero readers outside config.py for each: CASCADE_CONFIDENCE (config.py:332; tests/test_optimization.py:1743 even asserts it is excluded from sweep stages); ANOMALY_THRESHOLD (config.py:411) — dreaming/anomaly.py:106 flag_anomalous(threshold=0.35) exists but repair.py never calls it (it uses edge_anomaly_scores + select_repair_targets), yet docs/self-healing.md:28 lists ANOMALY_THRESHOLD as the feature's knob; MEMORY_MANAGER_DUP_COSINE (config.py:402) — classify_operation hardcodes dup_cosine=0.97 as a param default (dreaming/manager.py:61) and the enabled path raises FeatureNotImplementedError (manager.py:100); DREAM_RULE_MAX_LEN (config.py:703) — mine_rules has no max_len parameter (dreaming/rules.py:26); FLOW_SESSION_DECAY (config.py:650) — the documented session-boundary decay is unimplemented; SALIENCE_ALPHA (config.py:510) — documented as the 'replay priority alpha' coupling, but replay priority is surprise^w_s * need^w_n * (1-R)^w_r only (dreaming/replay.py:54-56), so an ablation over SALIENCE_ALPHA is guaranteed null; CONSOLIDATION_INTERVAL_SEC (config.py:713) — referenced only by docs/architecture.md as a prod cron period.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Delete the seven Settings fields (config.py shrinks by ~20 lines) and correct the three doc references (docs/self-healing.md ANOMALY_THRESHOLD row, GUARD sentence, docs/architecture.md interval note becomes 'prod-deferred, not a config knob'). Where a hardcoded parameter default duplicates a deleted knob (dup_cosine=0.97, threshold=0.35), leave the parameter — it is honest about being code, not config. Then add a config-hygiene test that iterates Settings dataclass fields and asserts each field name or its env literal appears in at least one non-config module, so future dead knobs fail CI at introduction.
confirmed.expected_impact: product + ablation credibility: every dead knob is a lever an operator or an ablation script can pull that provably does nothing; removing them prevents null ablations from being reported as 'component contributes 0pp'.

verdict.risk: Field deletion itself is near-zero risk: no code readers, no Settings(**dict) reconstruction, no fields(Settings) enumeration, and external .env files setting the env vars simply keep being ignored. Real risks are in the fix's second half: (1) the proposed config-hygiene CI test would fail at introduction because the seven are an undercount — guard_enabled/guard_min_delta_pp/guard_alpha (docs/self-healing.md claims GUARD_ENABLED gates bench/guard.py but guard.py reads no settings), oss_worm_retention_days, adbpg_dsn, gdb_endpoint are also unread, so it needs an allowlist for intentional prod-infra placeholders or a wider cleanup; (2) the token-presence heuristic is gameable — CASCADE_CONFIDENCE would pass it today because tests/test_optimization.py:1742 mentions the literal only to assert its exclusion from sweep stages, so the test must discount test-only/negative-assertion mentions or it certifies dead knobs as live; (3) deleting consolidation_interval_sec removes a documented external-infra interface (prod Function Compute cron period per docs/architecture.md:250,345) — the doc edit covers this but it is a product decision, and any out-of-repo deploy tooling that greps config.py for the knob would lose its anchor.

confirmed.title: REFLEX_RECALL_MIN_COVERAGE below ABSTENTION_THRESHOLD silently breaks the documented no-spurious-abstain invariant
confirmed.evidence: config.py:599-602 documents an invariant: a reflex hit's coverage 'is also the dense_score the candidate carries into answer(), so a hit is always >= abstention_threshold (no confident reflex hit can spuriously abstain on coverage)'. The code enforces only packet.coverage >= settings.reflex_min_coverage (engine.py:1005, same test at engine.py:714), and nothing anywhere validates reflex_min_coverage against abstention_threshold (default 0.65 vs 0.4 happens to satisfy it). Setting REFLEX_RECALL_MIN_COVERAGE=0.3 — a plausible recall-tuning move — silently produces reflex hits whose dense_score 0.3-0.4 falls under the coverage gate, so questions the full-retrieval path would have answered get abstained on the fast path.
confirmed.fix_sketch: In the hit test, use effective_min = max(settings.reflex_min_coverage, settings.abstention_threshold) (one-line change at engine.py:1005/714), or fail-loud in get_settings() when reflex_recall_enabled and reflex_min_coverage < abstention_threshold. Add a test constructing Settings with min_coverage 0.3 / threshold 0.4 asserting the reflex path either falls back to full retrieval or the config is rejected. The clamp only reduces reflex hits (more full retrievals), so it can never create a verified-wrong channel.
confirmed.expected_impact: accuracy: prevents a silent recall regression class (spurious abstentions on confident local hits) under legitimate tuning; abstain-safe by construction.

verdict.risk: The clamp silently overrides operator intent: setting REFLEX_RECALL_MIN_COVERAGE below ABSTENTION_THRESHOLD becomes a no-op, and raising ABSTENTION_THRESHOLD above 0.65 silently shrinks the reflex hit rate (latency regression vs intended tuning, never accuracy). Prefer the fail-loud/doctor-check variant or log the clamp, since config.py currently has no validation precedent and a silent clamp trades one invisible behavior for another. Also note the clamp restores the invariant only for the v1 coverage gate; abstention_v2's multi-signal confidence can still abstain a reflex hit, so the config comment should be scoped to the v1 gate when fixed.

confirmed.title: APP_ENV=prod claims OSS-WORM + AnalyticDB-PG + GDB, but ADBPG_DSN and GDB_ENDPOINT are read nowhere — prod silently runs SQLite and the local graph
confirmed.evidence: config.py:3-5 ('The ONLY thing that changes between APP_ENV=dev and prod is the storage/DB backend... Alibaba Cloud OSS-WORM + AnalyticDB-PG + GDB in prod') and .env.example:24-25 repeat the claim. Repo-wide, is_prod is branched on exactly once — eidetic/substrate.py:149 (OSS blob substrate). adbpg_dsn (config.py:734) and gdb_endpoint (config.py:735) have zero readers, and store.py/graph.py have no prod path, so APP_ENV=prod with a configured ADBPG_DSN writes immutable blobs to OSS while state and graph stay on local SQLite/hnswlib with no warning.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Fail loud instead of silently degrading: in get_settings() (or Store/Graph constructors), when is_prod and adbpg_dsn or gdb_endpoint is non-empty, raise FeatureNotImplementedError naming what actually switches in prod today (substrate only) — the same pattern manager.py:100 already uses for MEMORY_MANAGER. Delete the two dead fields or keep them behind that guard, and fix the config header + .env.example sentence to 'prod currently switches the substrate to OSS-WORM; DB/graph backends are dev-local'.
confirmed.expected_impact: product: prevents a prod deployment from silently keeping its source-of-truth state on a local SQLite file while the operator believes it is on AnalyticDB-PG/GDB — a data-durability surprise, not just a docs nit.

verdict.risk: Startup hard-fail for any deployment that already sets APP_ENV=prod plus the (previously inert) ADBPG_DSN/GDB_ENDPOINT vars; if the raise lives in get_settings() (lru_cached, called by every entrypoint) it also blocks read-only tools like doctor/CLI, so it should be scoped to Store/Graph construction or paired with an escape hatch. Docs edits are zero-risk. No effect on suite (no test sets APP_ENV=prod) or on answer-path integrity.

confirmed.title: Numeric env parsing is three-way inconsistent: empty-string values crash 40+ float/int fields, are defaulted by _get_int, and silently read as False by _get_bool
confirmed.evidence: _get_int (config.py:175-179) returns the default when an env var is set-but-empty, but the ~40 float fields use float(_get(NAME, default)) and four int fields use int(_get(...)) (e.g. COVE_QUESTIONS config.py:420, SPAN_NLI_MIN_CHARS config.py:428), which raise at Settings() construction: reproduced with FLOW_DECAY='' -> ValueError('could not convert string to float'). _get_bool (config.py:172) reads the same empty string as False. Compounding it, apply_metabolism_overlay uses 'if k not in e' (config.py:124), so a set-but-empty profile key (e.g. EXTRACT_CHUNKING= leaking from a shell or CI) blocks the METABOLISM_MODE profile value AND parses as False — a metabolism run silently missing one component while metabolism_mode reads true (the manifest records the raw '' as the only trace).
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add _get_float mirroring _get_int's empty-string-to-default behavior and route all float(_get(...))/int(_get(...)) call sites through the two helpers (mechanical, config-local). In apply_metabolism_overlay, treat set-but-empty as unset: 'if not e.get(k)' preserves the documented explicit-override-wins semantics (an explicit 0/value still wins) while stopping empty strings from punching silent holes in the profile. Add a test constructing Settings with every env var set to '' asserting no crash and defaults applied.
confirmed.expected_impact: integrity + robustness: eliminates a silent ablation hole (empty env var disables a profile component while METABOLISM_MODE=1 is recorded) and makes set-but-empty behave identically across all 100+ flags instead of crash/default/False depending on field type.

verdict.risk: Two regression surfaces: (1) mechanically routing 77 float/int call sites through helpers risks a transposed or mistyped default during conversion — must be pinned by the proposed construct-Settings-with-every-var-empty test plus the existing suite; (2) changing apply_metabolism_overlay from 'if k not in e' to 'if not e.get(k)' flips behavior for any external shell/CI runbook that uses FLAG= (set-but-empty) as a disable idiom under METABOLISM_MODE=1 — after the fix that component silently turns ON. No in-repo script uses that idiom (scripts/proof_slice.sh uses explicit =0, matching the documented ablation pattern at config.py:26), and the baseline mode-off path stays byte-identical since the overlay early-returns, so the risk is confined to undocumented external workflows.

confirmed.title: SMQE eagerly materializes the entire active corpus on every ask, even when the claims backend answers
confirmed.evidence: structured_recall loads active_records first (smqe/engine.py:79-81 calling store.active_records_at, which SELECTs and pydantic-validates every active record row, store.py:241-252) and claims second (smqe/engine.py:82-85), then execute_plan tries claims FIRST and touches records only on a claim miss (executor.py:18-21). structured_answer runs unconditionally on every ask (engine.py:964-967), so a 10k-record namespace pays 10k MemoryRecord.model_validate_json calls per question even for questions the indexed claims table answers, and even before the semantic vector-cache lookup (engine.py:983-990) gets a chance.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Make record materialization lazy: pass a records thunk (or a cached-per-ask lazy list) into execute_plan and only call store.active_records_at when execute_claim_op returns None. Replace the no_active_records early-return check (smqe/engine.py:108-113) with a cheap indexed EXISTS/count query (store.count with the bitemporal clause) instead of materializing rows to test emptiness. Behavior-preserving on general grounds: the same records are seen by record ops when they run; claims-answered questions simply skip a scan they never used.
confirmed.expected_impact: Per-ask latency on claims-answered questions drops from O(corpus) SQLite+pydantic work to two indexed queries; ask latency stops growing linearly with memory size for the structured path that every query traverses. No accuracy or integrity change.

verdict.refutation: Core defect confirmed, but expected impact is overstated on three counts. (1) The claim "per-ask latency drops to two indexed queries / stops growing linearly with memory size" is false: store.active_claims_at (store.py:378-396) still materializes and pydantic-validates EVERY active claim on every ask, and execute_claim_op iterates all of them (record_ops.py:4663-4666), so claims-answered asks remain O(#claims) even after the fix — laziness removes only one of two corpus-linear scans. (2) On claim-miss asks (most open-ended queries), execute_record_op needs the full records list anyway, so there are zero savings on that path. (3) "Runs unconditionally on every ask" is slightly off — the exact-hash cache hit (engine.py:957-960) and flag-gated false-premise gate return before SMQE; only the semantic (paraphrase) cache is behind it as claimed. The fix itself is verified safe: verify.py resolves supports via indexed store.get_record (verify.py:92), not the materialized list; execute_plan's only production caller is smqe/engine.py:114; tests pass concrete lists so a lazy-sequence (rather than a thunk signature change) avoids test churn; no test asserts on active_record_count.
verdict.risk: The replacement EXISTS/COUNT query must exactly match active_records_at's bitemporal+scope SQL clause — any mismatch flips answerable questions into wrong "no_active_records" abstentions (fail-closed, so accuracy loss but no verified-wrong channel). Snapshot skew: records would be loaded after claims/claim-op instead of before, so a concurrent write mid-ask could make active_record_count telemetry diverge from the actually-materialized list (minor in single-process SQLite). If execute_plan's records parameter becomes a thunk instead of a lazy sequence, tests/test_smqe.py callers passing plain lists break. active_record_count in the /api/structured_recall trace (api.py:235, engine.py:778) must keep its value via the count query, adding one indexed query per ask.

confirmed.title: Proof currency stops one step short of external auditability: content-hash chains are emitted but nothing can audit a proof tree end-to-end
confirmed.evidence: prove_answer emits content_hash/raw_uri/snippet per evidence item (proofs.py:28-44) but provenance_complete only checks the hash strings are non-empty (proofs.py:57). Substrate.verify(content_hash) exists and recomputes sha256 over stored bytes (substrate.py:56-58), yet no MCP tool, API route, or engine method walks a proof tree and re-verifies it (grep for audit_proof/verify_proof across the package returns nothing); the WORM substrate's 'externally citable photographic recall' claim is therefore not machine-checkable by a third party holding a proof.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add a pure, model-free audit_proof(proof: dict) -> dict in proofs.py (~60 lines): for each evidence item, substrate.get(content_hash), recompute sha256 (hash_ok), and check whitespace-normalized snippet containment in the decoded raw bytes (snippet_grounded), returning per-item pass/fail plus an aggregate audited=True only when every grounded item passes both. Expose it as an MCP tool and GET /api/audit_proof next to the existing truth_ledger route (api.py:249-265). Deterministic, read-only, zero tokens; fails loud on missing hashes.
confirmed.expected_impact: Turns the immutable-substrate moat into a demonstrable differentiator -- any answer can be re-audited against write-once bytes by an external party without trusting the engine -- at zero model cost and no answer-path change; also gives benchmarking an integrity artifact (auditable proof pass rate) that competitors without a CAS layer cannot produce.

verdict.refutation: The narrow gap is real (proofs.py:57 provenance_complete is only a hash-non-emptiness check; no function takes a proof dict and re-audits it in one call), but the headline claim is overstated on three counts. (1) An engine method DOES walk citations and re-verify hashes: engine.structured_recall (engine.py:791-817) calls substrate.verify per citation, computes proof_link_checks/immutable_proof, and fail-closes verified answers lacking full immutable proof; it is exposed via /api/structured_recall and MCP structured_recall — the finder's grep for audit_proof/verify_proof simply missed the different name. (2) Third-party machine-checkability already exists in the only genuinely trustless form: /api/raw/{content_hash} (api.py:297) and MCP get_raw (mcp_server.py:399, with verified_against_self) return verbatim raw bytes, so an external auditor holding a proof can recompute sha256 and check snippets client-side today; a server-side audit_proof still requires trusting the engine, so it adds one-call convenience, not trustlessness. (3) Corpus-level hash audit (snap_back_audit, engine.py:1926) already feeds bench integrity artifacts. Worse, the sketched snippet_grounded containment check is unsound as specced: described media (models.py:106 is_described — text describes non-text raw, so image/PDF snippets are never substrings of binary bytes), SMQE claim-backend snippets that are derived atoms legitimately NLI-entailed rather than verbatim (smqe/verify.py:115-131), and summary-derived snippets (retrieval.py:4437) would all report false audit failures on correct answers, making the proposed 'auditable proof pass rate' artifact anti-marketing unless the check is modality/derivation-aware.
verdict.risk: A public audit_proof endpoint that calls substrate.get(content_hash) per proof item bypasses the namespace scope guard /api/raw deliberately enforces (api.py:300-308: a hash from namespace A must not open — or confirm existence of — bytes in namespace B); per-hash pass/fail replies become a cross-namespace content-existence oracle unless the route is scope-filtered like get_raw. The naive snippet_grounded check would publish a low audit pass rate on legitimate derived/multimodal answers, damaging the integrity story it is meant to strengthen. Otherwise low regression risk: read-only, off the answer path, no record_ops growth, fails toward 'not audited' never toward verified-wrong.
